 ⏺ Six things to address. Going dense.

 ---

 1. Gmail spillover — don't do it. Here's why.

 The 5k/week Outreach cap is a user-level cap, not a mailbox-level cap. One user, multiple mailboxes → still 5k total. So you can't solve this by adding a mailbox.

 Three options exist and all have real problems:

 ┌────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Option │ Works? │ Tradeoffs │

 ├────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Send from Gmail outside │ Yes, bypasses │ Breaks: sequence automation, open/click tracking, reply detection in sequence state, prospect-state management, do-not-contact suppression. Outreach │

 │ Outreach │ Outreach's cap │ would not know those prospects were touched. Double-touch risk. Sender-reputation risk if Outreach-tracked and Gmail-direct sends use different │

 │ │ │ headers/auth on the same mailbox. │

 ├────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Route Outreach through │ Doesn't help │ Outreach already does this for Workspace accounts. The 5k/week cap is enforced at the Outreach layer regardless of what SMTP/API it uses underneath. │

 │ Gmail as the send channel │ │ You're not bypassing anything. │

 ├────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Second Outreach user │ Yes, gives │ Additional Outreach seat cost + second mailbox + second persona for replies — not useful for a single-rep workflow. │

 │ license │ 10k/week │ │

 ├────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Scout builds its own │ │ │

 │ sequence engine on Gmail │ Yes, would work │ Massive build. Fractures tracking between Scout and Outreach. Outreach stops being source-of-truth for sequence state. Bad idea. │

 │ API │ │ │

 └────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 My recommendation: don't spill over. Prioritize ruthlessly within 5k/week. 5k/week = ~700/day which is plenty for a single rep if the pipeline is well-targeted. The real answer to "I'm hitting the cap" is

 usually "the pipeline has too many low-probability prospects in sequences," not "I need more sending capacity." Scout's job (once the research engine is redesigned) is to make sure every one of those 5k

 emails/week is going to a high-signal target.

 ---

 2. Ground truth caveat — absorbed.

 Active Accounts / Pipeline contacts are sparse (1-2 per account, sometimes an invoicing/purchasing second contact you wouldn't target). That changes how I use them in A/B scoring. Specifically:

 - Active Accounts contacts are a "must-find" floor , not a recall ceiling. If an Active Account has a known CS Coordinator and neither engine finds them, both engines failed — that's a signal to look at the

 engine quality, not a score for either one.

 - Don't score engines on "how close did you come to the full set of Active Accounts contacts" — that set is too small to be a meaningful denominator.

 - Primary A/B metric stays "what the other engine finds." Engine-vs-engine diff is the core comparison. Active Accounts is a narrow sanity check layered on top.

 1. Primary: engine-vs-engine contact overlap + cost + time

 2. Secondary: per-contact quality (email verification status, title relevance, seniority)

 3. Sanity check: did both engines find any known Active Accounts / Pipeline contacts for this target? If no, flag the target for manual review rather than scoring.

 ---

 3. Drifting — I hear you. I'm going to fix this before we continue.

 You're right. I've been letting scanners and side-strategies pull me off-center. Committing to two things before we proceed with the design:

 1. Add a new task to this session: "Read full Scout strategy stack context" — pull up SCOUT_PLAN.md's strategy sections, SCOUT_HISTORY.md's stage/phase narrative, and every F1-F9 scanner's purpose so I can

 answer "what exists, why it was built, what learnings drove it, and how it fits the public-district prospecting motion" without hand-waving. I'll do that next, after I respond to the rest of this message.

 2. Bank a memory file now: feedback_scout_primary_target_is_public_districts.md — public school districts in your 13 territory states are the primary prospecting lane. Charter CMOs, CTE centers, diocesan

 systems, and private school networks are secondary scanners that feed the queue when relevant — they don't lead the design. Load this every session that touches strategy selection or A/B target selection.

 I'll do both as part of this session before moving to the design proposal phase.

 ---

 4. Volume + budget — you're right, I was sloppy. Redoing the math.

 You're correct: if one research job returns ~10 contacts on average, then at saturation you're running closer to 100 jobs/week, not 1,000. Current engine's per-job yield based on S58 diocesan runs is 10-18

 contacts/job (67% verified rate, 107 total across 6 archdioceses), so ~10-15 contacts/job is a fair baseline for public districts too.

 Real budget math:

 - $25/week max (Steven's actual number, lower is better)

 - ~100 atomic entities researched/week at saturation

 - Cost per atomic entity target: $0.25 absolute max, ideally $0.10-0.15

 - Current engine at $0.35-0.95 / atomic entity is 2-4x too expensive at the cheap end and 8-10x too expensive at the upper end

 - Target reduction: 3-6x cheaper (not "10x" — that was me inventing a number)

 Wall-clock target: still under 90 seconds per atomic entity when running interactively, so a "research FWISD" command returns within ~1-2 minutes.

 The cheaper number ($25/week) is easier to hit than the $50-150/week I was throwing around, because the volume is 10x lower than I was assuming. The per-job target is roughly the same ($0.10-0.15). So the

 design shape doesn't change dramatically — same optimizations, same quality bar — but the urgency is lower and the budget is harder, which is fine.

 ---

 5. Job definition — adopted.

 ▎ "Job" = 1 research/prospecting request, regardless of how many entities are underneath.

 Operationally:

 - /research FWISD → 1 job, 1 atomic entity

 - /research all private schools in OK → 1 job, ~50-150 atomic entities under the hood

 - /research all schools in LAUSD → 1 job, ~1,000 atomic entities under the hood

 - /research all CTE directors in Orange County CA → 1 job, ~10-15 atomic entities under the hood

 The engine internally still needs an "atomic entity" concept for caching, parallelism, and A/B comparison (you can't meaningfully cache or diff at the "one job" level if the job contains 150 entities). But the

 unit you see, the unit you think about, the unit Scout reports progress on, and the unit we budget against is the job.

 Progress reporting to you will look like:

 ▎ "Job: /research all private schools in OK — 47/112 entities processed, 18 min elapsed, 312 contacts found so far, est 9 min remaining, est cost so far $4.20"

 Not like:

 ▎ "Atomic entity 47 of 112 complete."

 One subtle implication: the $25/week budget applies to jobs, but the internal cost per atomic entity ($0.10-0.15) is what drives whether a large-fan-out job is affordable. A "research all schools in LAUSD" job

 would be ~$100-150 at target cost — you'd want Scout to warn you before running it, not silently burn a month of budget on one command. I'll build a pre-flight cost estimate + confirmation for large-fan-out

 jobs into the design.

 ---

 ⏺ Data's in. Two big corrections coming.

 ---

 Real public district yield (measurement, n=20 Research Log rows, diocesan/charter/CTE/private excluded):

 ┌────────────┬────────────────┬────────────┐

 │ Metric │ Total contacts │ With email │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ min │ 0 │ 0 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p25 │ 34 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ median │ 43 │ 5 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p75 │ 54 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p90 │ 82 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ max │ 90 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ mean │ 43.7 │ 21.9 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ zero-yield │ 5% (1/20) │ 10% (2/20) │

 └────────────┴────────────────┴────────────┘

 Sample rows to ground the numbers: Austin ISD 43/41, Coppell ISD 54/50, Mansfield ISD 49/1, Midway ISD 34/0, Columbus City Schools OH 90/83, Guthrie Public OK 52/52, Leander ISD 31/24, Friendswood ISD 31/2.

 My earlier "10-15 contacts/job" baseline was wrong. The real public-district mean is ~44 contacts/job total, ~22 with email. Retracting.

 But a bigger insight is hiding in the with-email column: median 5, mean 22 — that's a HUGE skew. Some districts return 80+ verified emails (Columbus, Austin, Guthrie, Coppell) and some return 0-2 (Midway,

 Mansfield, Friendswood). This isn't noise — it's a structural split:

 - "Easy" districts publish a real staff directory with mailto: links → engine scoops it up → 40-80 verified emails in one job

 - "Hard" districts hide contacts behind search forms / login walls / no directory at all → engine finds names from board minutes and state DOE docs but no emails → yield drops to 0-5 verified

 This matters for the redesign and I want to flag it now: the engine should detect easy vs hard at runtime and route accordingly. Easy districts can run on a cheap fast path (fewer layers, aggressive caching,

 batched Haiku extraction). Hard districts need the full depth treatment. One-size-fits-all is why the current engine is overspending on easy targets and undershooting on hard ones. The A/B test set should

 include both shapes so we measure across the variance.

 ---

 If median-with-email is the conservative floor (5) and mean-with-email is the optimistic ceiling (22):

 ┌────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬────────────────┬────────────────┐

 │ Scenario │ Jobs/week at saturation │ $0.50/job cost │ $0.15/job cost │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Optimistic (22 emails/job avg) │ ~49 jobs/week │ $25/week │ $7/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Realistic blend │ ~100 jobs/week │ $50/week │ $15/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Conservative (5 emails/job median) │ ~220 jobs/week │ $110/week │ $33/week │

 └────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────────┘

 At $25/week hard budget:

 - Optimistic case: current engine is already affordable ($0.50/job × 50 = $25)

 - Realistic blend: need ~2x cost reduction (~$0.25/job target)

 - Conservative case: need ~4-5x cost reduction (~$0.12/job target)

 So the real cost reduction target is 2-5x, not 10x. That's a materially less aggressive redesign than I was walking toward. Optimizations that were going to require quality tradeoffs (Haiku-everywhere,

 aggressive early termination, skipping Firecrawl) are off the table — the math says we don't need them. The achievable optimizations are:

 - Batch Claude calls (5-10 pages per call instead of one per page) → ~50% Claude cost cut on L9

 - Entity-level cache with 30-60 day TTL → 30-60% cost cut on repeat runs

 - Model swap to Haiku ONLY for high-confidence structured pages (staff directories with known format) → ~30% cost cut on easy-path jobs

 - Early termination only when confidence is very high (≥10 verified contacts above 0.9 confidence on an easy-path job) → skips L15-L20 on the easy jobs

 - Job-level parallelism with shared Serper/Exa/Brave token bucket → 3-5x speed up (wall clock), same cost

 These are all structural wins that preserve quality . They should hit the 2-5x cost target without any quality sacrifice. If real-use data later shows the conservative scenario is the truth and we need deeper

 cuts, we revisit then — but not on speculation.

 What changes with speed being negotiable:

 Two modes, not one. Engine gets a batch_mode flag:

 ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┬────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Mode │ When it runs │ Wall clock/job │ Parallelism │ Optimizations enabled │

 │ │ │ target │ │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Interactive (default for /research X ) │ On-demand, during │ < 90 sec │ 1 job at a time but layers │ Full depth, no aggressive batching, cache read-through │

 │ │ the day │ │ still parallel within the job │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Batch (default for campaigns with >10 │ Scheduled window, │ 5-10 min/job │ 4-way concurrent jobs with │ Full depth + Claude call batching (5-10 pages per call, saves ~50% on L9 cost — only │

 │ entities, OR user schedules overnight) │ 6pm-6am CST │ OK │ shared token bucket │ works when latency doesn't matter) + cache write-through + pre-warm territory_data │

 └───────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Why batching Claude calls is a batch-mode-only win: to batch 10 pages into one Claude call, the engine has to wait for all 10 pages to finish loading before firing the Claude request. Interactively, that's a

 30-60 second wall-clock penalty while you're staring at the progress bar. Overnight, it's free — and it's probably the single biggest cost-cut lever we have on L9 (the Claude extraction layer that's currently

 making one blocking call per page with no batching).

 Scheduling fits Scout's existing cron pattern. Scout already runs morning brief at 9:15am, EOD at 4:30pm, hourly check-ins 10am-4pm CST. Adding "overnight research batch drains the queue from 6pm to 6am" is

 the same pattern. When you kick off a big sweep, Scout says: "Queued 47 districts. Will run tonight starting 6pm CST, estimated cost $7, will report results in tomorrow's morning brief." With an override

 ( /research now <target> ) for jobs you actually need during the day.

 Critically: you're still not sacrificing quality. Both modes run the full 20-layer depth. The only differences are:

 1. Batch mode batches Claude calls (same extractions, just grouped differently in API calls — no change to what's extracted)

 2. Batch mode runs 4 jobs in parallel (same per-job behavior)

 3. Batch mode can pre-warm caches for the region (hitting fewer unique URLs)

 Cost implication: batching Claude alone probably drops per-job cost from $0.35-0.95 into the $0.20-0.55 range. Combined with entity cache hits on repeat runs and model swap on high-confidence pages only, we

 should land in the $0.12-0.30 per-job range — comfortably inside the $25/week budget at the realistic 100 jobs/week saturation level.

 ⏺ No problem. Q4 was jargon. Let me rewrite it in plain language and make it concrete.

 ---

 Q4 in plain English: the new research engine isn't going to be one big rewrite. It's going to be a list of 6-8 specific improvements , each one a separate piece of code I can build, test, and measure on its

 own. The question I'm asking is: which of those improvements do we build into the FIRST version of the new engine, and which do we leave for later?

 Why this matters: if I build all 8 at once, and the A/B test shows the new engine is 5% worse at finding contacts, we won't know WHICH change caused it. If I build them separately and turn them on one at a

 time, we can measure each one cleanly. But building fewer at once means a smaller cost win in round 1. There's a tradeoff and you get to pick where the line is.

 Here's the full menu with my read on each. Plain English, honest estimates labeled by provenance, my recommendation at the end of each row.

 ---

 The 8 possible improvements:

 1. Remember results for 30-60 days (entity cache).

 - What: After researching Cypress-Fairbanks ISD once, Scout remembers the contacts for 30-60 days. Research the same district again → instant, zero cost. You can force a fresh run with a --fresh flag.

 - Cost savings: big when you re-research anything (estimate: 30-60% of calls become free over time, depends on how much your targets overlap week-to-week)

 - Quality risk: none — you can always force fresh

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 2. Bundle Claude calls (batch extraction).

 - What: Today, the engine sends 10 separate requests to Claude for 10 web pages. The new version bundles all 10 pages into 1 request. Same contacts extracted, ~50% fewer API calls.

 - Cost savings: cuts the biggest single cost driver roughly in half (measurement: L9 Claude extraction is currently 40-60% of total per-job cost, this cuts it ~50%)

 - Quality risk: minimal if built carefully. Claude handles multi-doc inputs well.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (rewrite extract_from_multiple in tools/contact_extractor.py , A/B test against current golden outputs)

 - Catch: only works in batch mode / overnight runs — for interactive /research X requests, bundling means waiting for all 10 pages to load before firing Claude, which adds 30-60 seconds of wall clock.

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode

 3. Use Haiku (cheap model) on easy pages only.

 - What: When a page is clearly a structured staff directory (name/title/email in a table or list), use Claude Haiku 4.5 — ~10x cheaper. When a page is ambiguous (news article, board meeting minutes), keep

 Sonnet.

 - Cost savings: 20-30% on the Claude extraction cost, depends on what fraction of pages qualify as "easy structured" (unknown until we measure)

 - Quality risk: LOW if the page classifier is accurate. HIGHER if the classifier mislabels ambiguous pages as easy. Needs an A/B validation before it ships as default.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (classifier + model swap + validation)

 - My pick: BUILD but default OFF. Turn on later after the A/B proves it's safe. Measuring it in round 1 tells us whether it's worth shipping.

 4. Run 4 jobs at the same time (parallelism).

 - What: Today, ResearchQueue runs one job at a time to avoid Serper rate limits. The new version runs up to 4 jobs concurrently, using a shared rate-limit budget so they don't step on each other.

 - Cost savings: 0% (same total work, same API calls)

 - Speed savings: 3-4x wall clock on campaigns (a 50-district sweep goes from 4 hours serial to ~1 hour parallel)

 - Quality risk: minimal — if rate limits trip, jobs retry automatically

 - Build effort: ~2-3 days (token bucket + worker pool + retry logic)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode (interactive runs 1 job at a time by definition — you typed one command)

 5. Stop early when the engine already has enough.

 - What: If by layer 6 the engine has already found 10+ verified contacts with 0.9+ confidence on an easy target, skip layers 7-20. Current engine runs all 20 layers regardless.

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy jobs

 - Quality risk: MEDIUM — this is the one that could actually miss the long-tail contacts that only appear in L12-L20. Needs careful threshold tuning against A/B results before it's safe to default on.

 - Build effort: ~1 day (threshold check after each phase)

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Don't build it until we have real A/B data showing the threshold is safe. Otherwise we're cutting quality on speculation.

 6. Remember district domains (territory list as a shortcut).

 - What: Layer 4 today does ~6-8 Serper queries to discover a district's website domain (e.g., "cfisd.net" for Cypress-Fairbanks). The new version checks if we already discovered that domain in a previous run —

 if yes, skip L4 entirely.

 - Cost savings: 10-20% on any repeat district, 5-10% amortized

 - Quality risk: none (fall back to L4 if we don't have a cached domain)

 - Build effort: ~1 day (add a column to territory_data or sibling cache, lookup in L4)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 7. Detect "easy" vs "hard" districts and route differently.

 - What: After the first 3 layers, if we've found the district's staff directory URL, run a shorter fast-path (skip redundant search layers). If we haven't, run the full depth. This is what I noticed in the

 Research Log yield data — some districts return 80+ emails (easy), some return 0-5 (hard).

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy targets, 0% on hard ones

 - Quality risk: low if the "easy" detector is conservative. Ship the detector as "only take fast path when VERY confident we have the directory."

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Nice-to-have but not essential. Revisit after we see what % of targets are truly "easy" in the A/B data.

 8. Overnight batch scheduler.

 - What: When you kick off a campaign ( /sweep all districts in IL over 20k ), Scout says "queued 23 districts, running tonight 6pm-6am CST, results in tomorrow's morning brief." You get a one-line confirmation

 now and results in the morning. Small campaigns still run immediately if you want.

 - Cost savings: 0% direct, but this is what UNLOCKS improvement #2 (Claude batching) for campaign runs — batching only makes sense when latency is free, which it is overnight

 - Quality risk: none — just UX

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (scheduler hook + campaign shape detection + morning brief integration)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON for campaigns with >10 entities

 ---

 My recommended round 1 lineup:

 Ship these five:

 - #1 Cache (big win, no risk)

 - #2 Claude batching (biggest single cost cut, batch mode only)

 - #4 Job parallelism (campaigns go from hours to minutes)

 - #6 Territory domain shortcut (free win)

 - #8 Batch scheduler (unlocks #2 for campaigns)

 Build but default OFF:

 - #3 Haiku on easy pages (measure it in A/B, ship later if it passes)

 Skip for now:

 - #5 Early termination (too much quality risk without A/B data first)

 - #7 Easy/hard routing (nice-to-have, revisit later)

 Estimated round 1 impact (derived from the savings estimates above, labeled estimate — not measurement ):

 - Per-job cost: $0.35-0.95 today → $0.15-0.45 after round 1 (~2x reduction, lower end of the 2-5x target)

 - Wall clock for a 50-district campaign: ~4 hours serial → ~50 min parallel + batched

 - Interactive /research X latency: unchanged (still 3-8 min, because batching and parallelism don't apply to single-target runs)

 Round 1 build time estimate: ~6-10 working days of plan-mode + implementation + A/B harness build.

 ---

 Q4 restated in one sentence: Do you approve this round 1 lineup, or do you want to swap something in/out?

 Options:

 - (a) Approve as-is. I build the 5 ship items + #3 toggle, skip #5 and #7, and we run the A/B test after round 1 is done.

 - (b) Approve with modifications. Tell me which items you want added (e.g., "add #5, I'll take the risk") or removed (e.g., "don't bother with #6, too small a win for the complexity").

 - (c) Something's unclear. Tell me which one doesn't make sense and I'll explain it more.

 ❯ OK i think this method your seuggesting will take too long and be too many steps. we just need to start trying to figure things out. We should do our absolute best to examine everything we have look at

 everything carefully and creatively implement improvements and test as we go. We can do a really long in depth fix another time.

 ⏺ Heard. Reframing: skip the 8-item menu approach, skip the giant plan doc for later, actually dig into the code now and find specific wins I can point at.

 Here's what I'm about to do (should take ~15 min):

 1. Read tools/research_engine.py (all 1,795 lines) and tools/contact_extractor.py end-to-end — not skim, read. Look for actual inefficiencies, dead code, redundant queries, bad model choices, anything that's

 wasting money or time that I can point at with a file:line.

 2. Pull the Research Log notes field for the 20 public-district jobs I already found — see if layer-by-layer stats are in there, and if so which layers actually produce contacts vs which run and find nothing.

 3. Come back with 3-5 concrete improvements I've seen in the code , each with: what I saw, which file:line, why it's wasteful, what I'd change, and how risky the change is.

 Then we pick 1-2 to ship as quick wins, I build them, we A/B test against the current engine on one of the 5 targets, measure, iterate. Each win is a separate small commit, not a bundled rewrite.

 No more menu-style questions. Starting now.

 ⎿ tools/research_engine.py

 ⎿   ◼ Ask Steven clarifying questions one at a time

 ◻ Propose 2-3 approaches with tradeoffs

 ◻ Present design in sections for Steven's approval

 ◻ Write design doc to docs/superpowers/specs/

 ◻ Spec self-review (placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope)

 ◻ User reviews spec — Steven sign-off gate

 ◻ Transition to writing-plans skill

 ✔ Explore research engine context

 ✔ Bank public-districts + no-diocese-default memory files

 ✔ Read full Scout strategy stack context (F1-F9, stages, roadmap)

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one
 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,
 that's 20 separ te So net calls with identical system prompts.

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2
 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page
 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test
 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 94 + # Pull Research Log directly

 95 + from googleapiclient.discovery import build

 96 + from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials

 97 + import json as _json

 98 +

 99 + creds_json = os.environ.get( "GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON" )

 100 + creds_dict = _json.loads(creds_json)

 101 + creds = Credentials.from_service_account_info(

 102 + creds_dict, scopes=[ "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/spreadsheets" ]

 103 + )

 104 + svc = build( "sheets" , "v4" , credentials=creds)

 105 + sheet_id = os.environ.get( "GOOGLE_SHEETS_ID" )

 106 + result = svc.spreadsheets().values().get(

 107 + spreadsheetId=sheet_id, range = "'Research Log'!A2:I"

 108 + ).execute()

 109 + rows = result.get( "values" , [])

 110 + print ( f"Total Research Log rows: { len (rows)} " )

 111 +

 112 + # Exclude anything that looks diocesan / charter / CTE / private school network

 113 + def is_public_district ( name: str ) -> bool :

 114 + n = (name or "" ).lower()

 115 + bad_tokens = [

 116 + "archdiocese" , "diocese" , "catholic" , "parochial" , "cte center" ,

 117 + "career technology" , "career technical center" , "tech center" ,

 118 + "kipp" , "idea public schools" , "harmony public" , "aspire public" ,

 119 + "uplift education" , "responsiveed" , "great hearts" , "cristo rey" ,

 120 + "charter school" , "charter academy" , "charter network" ,

 121 + "academy of" , "prep academy" , " prep " , "archbishop" ,

 122 + ]

 123 + return not any (tok in n for tok in bad_tokens)

 124 +

 125 + public_rows = [r for r in rows if len (r) > 4 and is_public_district(r[ 1 ])]

 126 + print ( f"Public district rows (excluding diocesan/charter/CTE/private networks): { len (public_rows)} " )

 127 +

 128 + # Compute yield distribution

 129 + yields = []

 130 + with_email_yields = []

 131 + for r in public_rows:

 132 + try :

 133 + total = int (r[ 4 ]) if len (r) > 4 and r[ 4 ] else 0

 134 + with_em = int (r[ 5 ]) if len (r) > 5 and r[ 5 ] else 0

 135 + yields.append(total)

 136 + with_email_yields.append(with_em)

 137 + except (Value Error, Index Error):

 138 + continue

 139 +

 140 + if yields:

 141 + yields_sorted = sorted (yields)

 142 + n = len (yields)

 143 + print ( f"\nContacts-found distribution over {n} public district research jobs:" )

 144 + print ( f" min: {yields_sorted[ 0 ]} " )

 145 + print ( f" p25: {yields_sorted[n // 4 ]} " )

 146 + print ( f" median: {yields_sorted[n // 2 ]} " )

 147 + print ( f" p75: {yields_sorted[( 3 * n) // 4 ]} " )

 148 + print ( f" p90: {yields_sorted[ min (n - 1 , ( 9 * n) // 10 )]} " )

 149 + print ( f" max: {yields_sorted[- 1 ]} " )

 150 + print ( f" mean: { sum (yields) / n: .1 f} " )

 151 + print ( f" zero-yield jobs: { sum ( 1 for y in yields if y == 0 )} ( { 100 * sum ( 1 for y in yields if y == 0 ) / n: .0 f} %)" )

 152 +

 153 + em_sorted = sorted (with_email_yields)

 154 + print ( f"\nContacts-with-email distribution:" )

 155 + print ( f" median: {em_sorted[n // 2 ]} " )

 156 + print ( f" mean: { sum (with_email_yields) / n: .1 f} " )

 157 + print ( f" zero-email jobs: { sum ( 1 for y in with_email_yields if y == 0 )} ( { 100 * sum ( 1 for y in with_email_yields if y == 0 ) / n: .0 f} %)" )

 158 + else :

 159 + print ( "No public district jobs in Research Log" )

 160 +

 163 + print ( f"\nSample public district rows (first 5):" )

 168 + print ( f"\nSample public district rows (last 5):" )

 172 + print ( f" {r[ 0 ]} | {r[ 1 ]} ( {r[ 2 ] if len (r) > 2 else '?' } ) | total= {total} with_email= {with_em} " )

 173 +

 174 + print ()

 … +65 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Data's in. Two big corrections coming.

 ---

 Real public district yield (measurement, n=20 Research Log rows, diocesan/charter/CTE/private excluded):

 ┌────────────┬────────────────┬────────────┐

 │ Metric │ Total contacts │ With email │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ min │ 0 │ 0 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p25 │ 34 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ median │ 43 │ 5 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p75 │ 54 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p90 │ 82 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ max │ 90 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ mean │ 43.7 │ 21.9 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ zero-yield │ 5% (1/20) │ 10% (2/20) │

 └────────────┴────────────────┴────────────┘

 Sample rows to ground the numbers: Austin ISD 43/41, Coppell ISD 54/50, Mansfield ISD 49/1, Midway ISD 34/0, Columbus City Schools OH 90/83, Guthrie Public OK 52/52, Leander ISD 31/24, Friendswood ISD 31/2.

 My earlier "10-15 contacts/job" baseline was wrong. The real public-district mean is ~44 contacts/job total, ~22 with email. Retracting.

 But a bigger insight is hiding in the with-email column: median 5, mean 22 — that's a HUGE skew. Some districts return 80+ verified emails (Columbus, Austin, Guthrie, Coppell) and some return 0-2 (Midway,

 Mansfield, Friendswood). This isn't noise — it's a structural split:

 - "Easy" districts publish a real staff directory with mailto: links → engine scoops it up → 40-80 verified emails in one job

 - "Hard" districts hide contacts behind search forms / login walls / no directory at all → engine finds names from board minutes and state DOE docs but no emails → yield drops to 0-5 verified

 This matters for the redesign and I want to flag it now: the engine should detect easy vs hard at runtime and route accordingly. Easy districts can run on a cheap fast path (fewer layers, aggressive caching,

 batched Haiku extraction). Hard districts need the full depth treatment. One-size-fits-all is why the current engine is overspending on easy targets and undershooting on hard ones. The A/B test set should

 include both shapes so we measure across the variance.

 ---

 If median-with-email is the conservative floor (5) and mean-with-email is the optimistic ceiling (22):

 ┌────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬────────────────┬────────────────┐

 │ Scenario │ Jobs/week at saturation │ $0.50/job cost │ $0.15/job cost │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Optimistic (22 emails/job avg) │ ~49 jobs/week │ $25/week │ $7/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Realistic blend │ ~100 jobs/week │ $50/week │ $15/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Conservative (5 emails/job median) │ ~220 jobs/week │ $110/week │ $33/week │

 └────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────────┘

 At $25/week hard budget:

 - Optimistic case: current engine is already affordable ($0.50/job × 50 = $25)

 - Realistic blend: need ~2x cost reduction (~$0.25/job target)

 - Conservative case: need ~4-5x cost reduction (~$0.12/job target)

 So the real cost reduction target is 2-5x, not 10x. That's a materially less aggressive redesign than I was walking toward. Optimizations that were going to require quality tradeoffs (Haiku-everywhere,

 aggressive early termination, skipping Firecrawl) are off the table — the math says we don't need them. The achievable optimizations are:

 - Batch Claude calls (5-10 pages per call instead of one per page) → ~50% Claude cost cut on L9

 - Entity-level cache with 30-60 day TTL → 30-60% cost cut on repeat runs

 - Model swap to Haiku ONLY for high-confidence structured pages (staff directories with known format) → ~30% cost cut on easy-path jobs

 - Early termination only when confidence is very high (≥10 verified contacts above 0.9 confidence on an easy-path job) → skips L15-L20 on the easy jobs

 - Job-level parallelism with shared Serper/Exa/Brave token bucket → 3-5x speed up (wall clock), same cost

 These are all structural wins that preserve quality . They should hit the 2-5x cost target without any quality sacrifice. If real-use data later shows the conservative scenario is the truth and we need deeper

 cuts, we revisit then — but not on speculation.

 ---

 Active Accounts check on the 5 candidates (measurement, n=176 Active Accounts across all states):

 All 5 candidates are cold — no district-level match, no school-level accounts underneath. Your territory has:

 ┌─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐

 │ State │ Active Accounts in that state │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ TX │ 31 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ CA │ 51 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ IL │ 21 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ OH │ 9 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ NE │ 4 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ (all 13 territory states total) │ 176 │

 └─────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

 None of: Cypress-Fairbanks ISD, Cincinnati Public Schools, Conejo Valley USD, Park Ridge-Niles CCSD 64, Waverly School District 145 have any active account at the district level OR at any school underneath

 them (per fuzzy name match at threshold 0.7). So we're running the A/B on 5 fully-cold targets with no Salesforce signal for ground truth. Pure engine-vs-engine comparison plus per-contact quality scoring.

 Caveat: my fuzzy-match threshold is conservative (0.7). If you think any of the 5 SHOULD have been caught (e.g. Cincinnati, which is a major district), tell me and I'll re-run with a looser match. But I don't

 think you have accounts there — the OH total is only 9, which bounds the possibilities.

 ---

 Where that leaves us:

 vs hard" split is a new insight that shapes the design.

 1. Write the public-districts memory ( feedback_scout_primary_target_is_public_districts.md )

 2. Write the diocesan-overreference memory ( feedback_dont_default_to_diocese_examples.md )

 3. Read SCOUT_PLAN.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md stage sections to load the full strategy stack context (F1-F9 purposes, Session 52 stage progression, what's been built and why)

 4. Verify the 5 test districts against territory_data (confirm in-territory, size tier accurate)

 5. THEN present the next decision point (Q4) — which optimizations go on the A/B menu, in what order, with what toggle granularity

 Anything in the yield data or the corrected cost math you want me to rework before I proceed? Otherwise I start step 1 now.

 ❯ Something i am wiling to sacrifice a bit is the speed (for now) i could potenetially schedule some of the seraches for after work hours and overnight.

 ⏺ Good constraint. Absorbed and it simplifies the design significantly.

 ---

 What changes with speed being negotiable:

 Two modes, not one. Engine gets a batch_mode flag:

 ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┬────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Mode │ When it runs │ Wall clock/job │ Parallelism │ Optimizations enabled │

 │ │ │ target │ │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Interactive (default for /research X ) │ On-demand, during │ < 90 sec │ 1 job at a time but layers │ Full depth, no aggressive batching, cache read-through │

 │ │ the day │ │ still parallel within the job │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Batch (default for campaigns with >10 │ Scheduled window, │ 5-10 min/job │ 4-way concurrent jobs with │ Full depth + Claude call batching (5-10 pages per call, saves ~50% on L9 cost — only │

 │ entities, OR user schedules overnight) │ 6pm-6am CST │ OK │ shared token bucket │ works when latency doesn't matter) + cache write-through + pre-warm territory_data │

 └───────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Why batching Claude calls is a batch-mode-only win: to batch 10 pages into one Claude call, the engine has to wait for all 10 pages to finish loading before firing the Claude request. Interactively, that's a

 30-60 second wall-clock penalty while you're staring at the progress bar. Overnight, it's free — and it's probably the single biggest cost-cut lever we have on L9 (the Claude extraction layer that's currently

 making one blocking call per page with no batching).

 Scheduling fits Scout's existing cron pattern. Scout already runs morning brief at 9:15am, EOD at 4:30pm, hourly check-ins 10am-4pm CST. Adding "overnight research batch drains the queue from 6pm to 6am" is

 the same pattern. When you kick off a big sweep, Scout says: "Queued 47 districts. Will run tonight starting 6pm CST, estimated cost $7, will report results in tomorrow's morning brief." With an override

 ( /research now <target> ) for jobs you actually need during the day.

 Critically: you're still not sacrificing quality. Both modes run the full 20-layer depth. The only differences are:

 1. Batch mode batches Claude calls (same extractions, just grouped differently in API calls — no change to what's extracted)

 2. Batch mode runs 4 jobs in parallel (same per-job behavior)

 3. Batch mode can pre-warm caches for the region (hitting fewer unique URLs)

 Cost implication: batching Claude alone probably drops per-job cost from $0.35-0.95 into the $0.20-0.55 range. Combined with entity cache hits on repeat runs and model swap on high-confidence pages only, we

 should land in the $0.12-0.30 per-job range — comfortably inside the $25/week budget at the realistic 100 jobs/week saturation level.

 ---

 Now proceeding with my committed next actions. Going to execute and report back, no more questions until done.

 Park Ridge miss — it's a real IL district but NCES stores the name differently and my fuzzy match didn't catch it. Rather than spend time chasing the NCES-specific name, I'm replacing it with a confirmed IL

 small-tier district from the territory list. Proposed swap:

 → Lake Zurich CUSD 95 (IL, 5,703 enrollment) — suburban Chicago, confirmed in territory. Natural "small" candidate. Keeps IL represented in the test set.

 Alternative IL small-tier candidates if you prefer something else (all confirmed in territory): Collinsville CUSD 10 (5,966), Comm Cons SD 59 (5,869), Granite City CUSD 9 (5,775), Lake Zurich CUSD 95 (5,703),

 Alton CUSD 11 (5,660).

 Secondary-lane scanners (feed the queue but NOT primary):

 │ ID │ Name │ Purpose │ Why secondary │

 The redesign's primary lens is the public-district primary lane (F1, F2, F3, F4, F9, upward, winback, cold_license_request, proximity). Secondary-lane scanners (F6, F7, F8, F10) benefit from the same engine

 but aren't the design target.

 All 5 A/B test targets map cleanly to this primary lane — they're the kind of districts that F1/F2/F3/F4/F9/upward/winback/cold_license_request feed into the queue. That's the right test set.

 ---

 Everything committed is done. Memories banked, strategy stack loaded, test set verified and adjusted, speed-negotiable constraint absorbed, batch-mode architecture sketched.

 Two things left before the design phase proper:

 1. Your sign-off on the Lake Zurich swap (or tell me to use Collinsville / Comm Cons 59 / a different IL district instead)

 2. Q4 when you're ready — which optimizations go on the A/B menu, in what order, with what toggle granularity. I'll frame it as a concrete list with cost/quality/build-effort estimates so you can pick.

 What's your call on #1, and ready for Q4?

 ❯ 1. good with that. 2. explain i dont understand what your asking

 ⏺ No problem. Q4 was jargon. Let me rewrite it in plain language and make it concrete.

 ---

 Q4 in plain English: the new research engine isn't going to be one big rewrite. It's going to be a list of 6-8 specific improvements , each one a separate piece of code I can build, test, and measure on its

 own. The question I'm asking is: which of those improvements do we build into the FIRST version of the new engine, and which do we leave for later?

 Why this matters: if I build all 8 at once, and the A/B test shows the new engine is 5% worse at finding contacts, we won't know WHICH change caused it. If I build them separately and turn them on one at a

 time, we can measure each one cleanly. But building fewer at once means a smaller cost win in round 1. There's a tradeoff and you get to pick where the line is.

 Here's the full menu with my read on each. Plain English, honest estimates labeled by provenance, my recommendation at the end of each row.

 ---

 The 8 possible improvements:

 1. Remember results for 30-60 days (entity cache).

 - What: After researching Cypress-Fairbanks ISD once, Scout remembers the contacts for 30-60 days. Research the same district again → instant, zero cost. You can force a fresh run with a --fresh flag.

 - Cost savings: big when you re-research anything (estimate: 30-60% of calls become free over time, depends on how much your targets overlap week-to-week)

 - Quality risk: none — you can always force fresh

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 2. Bundle Claude calls (batch extraction).

 - What: Today, the engine sends 10 separate requests to Claude for 10 web pages. The new version bundles all 10 pages into 1 request. Same contacts extracted, ~50% fewer API calls.

 - Cost savings: cuts the biggest single cost driver roughly in half (measurement: L9 Claude extraction is currently 40-60% of total per-job cost, this cuts it ~50%)

 - Quality risk: minimal if built carefully. Claude handles multi-doc inputs well.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (rewrite extract_from_multiple in tools/contact_extractor.py , A/B test against current golden outputs)

 - Catch: only works in batch mode / overnight runs — for interactive /research X requests, bundling means waiting for all 10 pages to load before firing Claude, which adds 30-60 seconds of wall clock.

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode

 3. Use Haiku (cheap model) on easy pages only.

 - What: When a page is clearly a structured staff directory (name/title/email in a table or list), use Claude Haiku 4.5 — ~10x cheaper. When a page is ambiguous (news article, board meeting minutes), keep

 Sonnet.

 - Cost savings: 20-30% on the Claude extraction cost, depends on what fraction of pages qualify as "easy structured" (unknown until we measure)

 - Quality risk: LOW if the page classifier is accurate. HIGHER if the classifier mislabels ambiguous pages as easy. Needs an A/B validation before it ships as default.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (classifier + model swap + validation)

 - My pick: BUILD but default OFF. Turn on later after the A/B proves it's safe. Measuring it in round 1 tells us whether it's worth shipping.

 4. Run 4 jobs at the same time (parallelism).

 - What: Today, ResearchQueue runs one job at a time to avoid Serper rate limits. The new version runs up to 4 jobs concurrently, using a shared rate-limit budget so they don't step on each other.

 - Cost savings: 0% (same total work, same API calls)

 - Speed savings: 3-4x wall clock on campaigns (a 50-district sweep goes from 4 hours serial to ~1 hour parallel)

 - Quality risk: minimal — if rate limits trip, jobs retry automatically

 - Build effort: ~2-3 days (token bucket + worker pool + retry logic)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode (interactive runs 1 job at a time by definition — you typed one command)

 5. Stop early when the engine already has enough.

 - What: If by layer 6 the engine has already found 10+ verified contacts with 0.9+ confidence on an easy target, skip layers 7-20. Current engine runs all 20 layers regardless.

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy jobs

 - Quality risk: MEDIUM — this is the one that could actually miss the long-tail contacts that only appear in L12-L20. Needs careful threshold tuning against A/B results before it's safe to default on.

 - Build effort: ~1 day (threshold check after each phase)

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Don't build it until we have real A/B data showing the threshold is safe. Otherwise we're cutting quality on speculation.

 6. Remember district domains (territory list as a shortcut).

 - What: Layer 4 today does ~6-8 Serper queries to discover a district's website domain (e.g., "cfisd.net" for Cypress-Fairbanks). The new version checks if we already discovered that domain in a previous run —

 if yes, skip L4 entirely.

 - Cost savings: 10-20% on any repeat district, 5-10% amortized

 - Quality risk: none (fall back to L4 if we don't have a cached domain)

 - Build effort: ~1 day (add a column to territory_data or sibling cache, lookup in L4)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 7. Detect "easy" vs "hard" districts and route differently.

 - What: After the first 3 layers, if we've found the district's staff directory URL, run a shorter fast-path (skip redundant search layers). If we haven't, run the full depth. This is what I noticed in the

 Research Log yield data — some districts return 80+ emails (easy), some return 0-5 (hard).

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy targets, 0% on hard ones

 - Quality risk: low if the "easy" detector is conservative. Ship the detector as "only take fast path when VERY confident we have the directory."

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Nice-to-have but not essential. Revisit after we see what % of targets are truly "easy" in the A/B data.

 8. Overnight batch scheduler.

 - What: When you kick off a campaign ( /sweep all districts in IL over 20k ), Scout says "queued 23 districts, running tonight 6pm-6am CST, results in tomorrow's morning brief." You get a one-line confirmation

 now and results in the morning. Small campaigns still run immediately if you want.

 - Cost savings: 0% direct, but this is what UNLOCKS improvement #2 (Claude batching) for campaign runs — batching only makes sense when latency is free, which it is overnight

 - Quality risk: none — just UX

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (scheduler hook + campaign shape detection + morning brief integration)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON for campaigns with >10 entities

 ---

 My recommended round 1 lineup:

 Ship these five:

 - #1 Cache (big win, no risk)

 - #2 Claude batching (biggest single cost cut, batch mode only)

 - #4 Job parallelism (campaigns go from hours to minutes)

 - #6 Territory domain shortcut (free win)

 - #8 Batch scheduler (unlocks #2 for campaigns)

 Build but default OFF:

 - #3 Haiku on easy pages (measure it in A/B, ship later if it passes)

 Skip for now:

 - #5 Early termination (too much quality risk without A/B data first)

 - #7 Easy/hard routing (nice-to-have, revisit later)

 Estimated round 1 impact (derived from the savings estimates above, labeled estimate — not measurement ):

 - Per-job cost: $0.35-0.95 today → $0.15-0.45 after round 1 (~2x reduction, lower end of the 2-5x target)

 - Wall clock for a 50-district campaign: ~4 hours serial → ~50 min parallel + batched

 - Interactive /research X latency: unchanged (still 3-8 min, because batching and parallelism don't apply to single-target runs)

 Round 1 build time estimate: ~6-10 working days of plan-mode + implementation + A/B harness build.

 ---

 Q4 restated in one sentence: Do you approve this round 1 lineup, or do you want to swap something in/out?

 Options:

 - (a) Approve as-is. I build the 5 ship items + #3 toggle, skip #5 and #7, and we run the A/B test after round 1 is done.

 - (b) Approve with modifications. Tell me which items you want added (e.g., "add #5, I'll take the risk") or removed (e.g., "don't bother with #6, too small a win for the complexity").

 - (c) Something's unclear. Tell me which one doesn't make sense and I'll explain it more.

 ❯ OK i think this method your seuggesting will take too long and be too many steps. we just need to start trying to figure things out. We should do our absolute best to examine everything we have look at

 everything carefully and creatively implement improvements and test as we go. We can do a really long in depth fix another time.

 ⏺ Heard. Reframing: skip the 8-item menu approach, skip the giant plan doc for later, actually dig into the code now and find specific wins I can point at.

 Here's what I'm about to do (should take ~15 min):

 1. Read tools/research_engine.py (all 1,795 lines) and tools/contact_extractor.py end-to-end — not skim, read. Look for actual inefficiencies, dead code, redundant queries, bad model choices, anything that's

 wasting money or time that I can point at with a file:line.

 2. Pull the Research Log notes field for the 20 public-district jobs I already found — see if layer-by-layer stats are in there, and if so which layers actually produce contacts vs which run and find nothing.

 3. Come back with 3-5 concrete improvements I've seen in the code , each with: what I saw, which file:line, why it's wasteful, what I'd change, and how risky the change is.

 Then we pick 1-2 to ship as quick wins, I build them, we A/B test against the current engine on one of the 5 targets, measure, iterate. Each win is a separate small commit, not a bundled rewrite.

 No more menu-style questions. Starting now.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Done reading. Here are the specific wins I found, each with file:line and honest estimates.

 ---

 5 concrete wins I can point at in the code

 Win #1 — L18 and L19 are BOTH running Firecrawl on the same district site (waste)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1095-1266

 L18 ( _layer18_firecrawl_extract ) calls fc.extract(urls=[f"https://{domain}/*"], enable_web_search=True) which crawls the district site AND does a web search, then extracts contacts directly (bypassing Claude).

 L19 ( _layer19_firecrawl_site_map ) separately calls fc.map() + up to 8 fc.scrape() calls on the same district site, then the scraped markdown flows into L9 Claude extraction.

 Both target the same domain with overlapping purpose. L18 and L19 are redundant — either one of them (if it works) covers the staff directory case. Running both means paying Firecrawl twice for the same

 district.

 Plus: L18 has enable_web_search=True set (line 1150), which doubles its cost. Web search is already done by L1/L2/L3/L5/L11/L14/L16/L20 — adding another web search pass inside Firecrawl is redundant.

 Fix: pick ONE. I'd keep L19 (map + scrape → raw markdown → L9 Claude extraction with full context visibility) and disable L18 entirely, OR if L18 stays, set enable_web_search=False .

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.20/job (estimate — Firecrawl's /extract with enable_web_search=True is one of their more expensive operations; exact savings depend on their current pricing which I'd verify before

 shipping). Risk: zero — if L19 alone is insufficient we can flip back.

 ---

 Win #2 — raw_pages is never URL-deduped before L9 Claude extraction

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:_add_raw_from_serper at line 1566, and the raw_pages.append(...) calls scattered through L1-L20.

 Multiple layers can return the SAME URL for the same district:

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 - L11 school-staff search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page (same URL)

 - L16 Exa broad: Austin ISD → possibly same page

 - L4 district-site search: also sometimes

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one

 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Fix: dedupe self.raw_pages by URL before L9 runs. ~5 lines of code at the top of _layer9_claude_extraction :

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which I haven't measured. Could verify with a one-line logger.info before shipping). Risk: zero — removes only

 duplicate work, no quality change.

 ---

 Win #3 — L9 calls Claude Sonnet one page at a time, no batching

 Where: tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 def extract_from_multiple(pages: list [ tuple [ str , str ]], district_name: str ) -> list [ dict ]:

 all_contacts = []

 seen = set ()

 for url, content in pages:

 contacts = extract_contacts(content, url, district_name) # ONE Sonnet call per page

 ...

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,

 that's 20 separate Sonnet calls with identical system prompts.

 Per-call overhead: 400 system tokens × 20 calls = 8,000 tokens of redundant prompt input (~$0.024 wasted on input alone).

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2

 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page

 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test

 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Caveat Steven already flagged: batching adds latency to interactive /research X (wait for all 5 pages before calling Claude) — so this should be batch_mode=True only for the overnight path, and the interactive

 path keeps single-page calls. Gated.

 ---

 Win #4 — L15 Step 5 "discovery" runs unconditionally even when we already have plenty

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1487-1519

 L15's Step 5 does 5 @domain Serper queries to find MORE contacts we might have missed (L1-L14 output), then feeds the results through another extract_from_multiple Claude call. Runs every time, regardless of

 how many VERIFIED contacts L9 already found.

 For "easy" districts where L9 already returned 20+ VERIFIED contacts, Step 5 is searching for more contacts we don't need — marginal yield is typically 0-2 extras.

 Fix: conditional skip. If all_contacts already has ≥10 contacts with email_confidence=VERIFIED (or some threshold you're comfortable with), skip Step 5. Keep Steps 1-4 (those improve existing contacts, not

 just pad the list).

 # ── Step 5: Discovery searches using @domain pattern ─────────────────

 if domain and _l15_used < _L15_MAX and self._serper_count < self._serper_cap:

 verified_count = sum ( 1 for c in self.all_contacts if c.get( 'email_confidence' ) == 'VERIFIED' )

 if verified_count >= 10 :

 logger.info( f"L15 Step 5 skipped: already have {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts" )

 else :

 remaining = min (_L15_MAX - _l15_used, 5 )

 ...

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1-2 Claude batches per "easy" job = ~$0.02-0.05/job on easy targets only (estimate — only applies when the threshold triggers). Risk: very low — you explicitly set the

 "we have enough" threshold.

 ---

 Win #5 — _merge_contacts silently drops higher-quality duplicates (THIS IS A QUALITY BUG, not a cost bug)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 AND tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 2 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, john.smith@district.edu, VERIFIED" — the page 2 record is DROPPED because John Smith is already in the seen set from page 1.

 This is a silent quality loss. Real scenario: L1 staff directory page has names but no emails. L11 school-staff page has the same name WITH email. Current code keeps the no-email version from L1.

 Fix: upgrade-on-merge logic. If a new contact has the same name as an existing one but has BETTER data (email when existing had none, VERIFIED when existing was INFERRED, etc.), REPLACE the existing record.

 def _merge_contacts(self, new_contacts: list [ dict ]):

 existing_by_key = {}

 for c in self.all_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 conf_rank = { "VERIFIED" : 4 , "LIKELY" : 3 , "INFERRED" : 2 , "UNKNOWN" : 1 , "" : 0 }

 for c in new_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 if key not in existing_by_key:

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 self.all_contacts.append(c)

 else :

 old = existing_by_key[key]

 old_conf = conf_rank.get(old.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 new_conf = conf_rank.get(c.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 # Upgrade if new has email and old didn't, or new has better confidence

 if (c.get( 'email' ) and not old.get( 'email' )) or new_conf > old_conf:

 old.update({k: v for k, v in c.items() if v})

 Estimated impact: ZERO cost savings, but non-trivial quality gain — potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Worth doing because "as good as possible" is your bar.

 Risk: very low — upgrading is strictly better than dropping.

 ---

 My recommended order to ship

 1. Win #2 (URL dedup) — tiny diff, zero risk, measurable savings immediately. ~30 min work.

 2. Win #5 (merge-upgrade) — tiny diff, pure quality gain. ~45 min work.

 3. Win #1 (L18 disable or enable_web_search=False ) — one-line change, measurable savings. ~15 min work.

 4. Win #4 (L15 Step 5 skip-when-enough) — ~10 line conditional. ~30 min work.

 5. Win #3 (L9 batching) — biggest cost win but requires careful rewrite + validation. ~half day work, gated to batch mode only.

 Wins 1-4 together: ~2 hours of work, estimated ~$0.15-0.40/job savings, zero quality risk, plus recovers some lost VERIFIED emails.

 Win 3 alone: ~half day work, estimated additional ~$0.10-0.30/job savings, low risk with A/B validation, batch-mode only.

 ---

 My proposal: start with Wins #1, #2, #4, and #5 right now as separate small commits. They're surgical, low-risk, and we can measure the combined effect by running one of the 5 test targets through both engines

 (old commit vs new commit on the same target).

 Then we evaluate, and if the numbers look good, we take on Win #3 as a slightly bigger piece of work.

 Do you want me to start building Wins #1/#2/#4/#5 as 4 small commits right now? Or adjust the order / add / remove anything first?

 ACTIVE ACCOUNTS LOOKUP (5 candidate test targets)

 … +65 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Data's in. Two big corrections coming.

 ---

 Real public district yield (measurement, n=20 Research Log rows, diocesan/charter/CTE/private excluded):

 ┌────────────┬────────────────┬────────────┐

 │ Metric │ Total contacts │ With email │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ min │ 0 │ 0 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p25 │ 34 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ median │ 43 │ 5 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p75 │ 54 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ p90 │ 82 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ max │ 90 │ — │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ mean │ 43.7 │ 21.9 │

 ├────────────┼────────────────┼────────────┤

 │ zero-yield │ 5% (1/20) │ 10% (2/20) │

 └────────────┴────────────────┴────────────┘

 Sample rows to ground the numbers: Austin ISD 43/41, Coppell ISD 54/50, Mansfield ISD 49/1, Midway ISD 34/0, Columbus City Schools OH 90/83, Guthrie Public OK 52/52, Leander ISD 31/24, Friendswood ISD 31/2.

 My earlier "10-15 contacts/job" baseline was wrong. The real public-district mean is ~44 contacts/job total, ~22 with email. Retracting.

 But a bigger insight is hiding in the with-email column: median 5, mean 22 — that's a HUGE skew. Some districts return 80+ verified emails (Columbus, Austin, Guthrie, Coppell) and some return 0-2 (Midway,

 Mansfield, Friendswood). This isn't noise — it's a structural split:

 - "Easy" districts publish a real staff directory with mailto: links → engine scoops it up → 40-80 verified emails in one job

 - "Hard" districts hide contacts behind search forms / login walls / no directory at all → engine finds names from board minutes and state DOE docs but no emails → yield drops to 0-5 verified

 This matters for the redesign and I want to flag it now: the engine should detect easy vs hard at runtime and route accordingly. Easy districts can run on a cheap fast path (fewer layers, aggressive caching,

 batched Haiku extraction). Hard districts need the full depth treatment. One-size-fits-all is why the current engine is overspending on easy targets and undershooting on hard ones. The A/B test set should

 include both shapes so we measure across the variance.

 ---

 If median-with-email is the conservative floor (5) and mean-with-email is the optimistic ceiling (22):

 ┌────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬────────────────┬────────────────┐

 │ Scenario │ Jobs/week at saturation │ $0.50/job cost │ $0.15/job cost │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Optimistic (22 emails/job avg) │ ~49 jobs/week │ $25/week │ $7/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Realistic blend │ ~100 jobs/week │ $50/week │ $15/week │

 ├────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼────────────────┼────────────────┤

 │ Conservative (5 emails/job median) │ ~220 jobs/week │ $110/week │ $33/week │

 └────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴────────────────┴────────────────┘

 At $25/week hard budget:

 - Optimistic case: current engine is already affordable ($0.50/job × 50 = $25)

 - Realistic blend: need ~2x cost reduction (~$0.25/job target)

 - Conservative case: need ~4-5x cost reduction (~$0.12/job target)

 So the real cost reduction target is 2-5x, not 10x. That's a materially less aggressive redesign than I was walking toward. Optimizations that were going to require quality tradeoffs (Haiku-everywhere,

 aggressive early termination, skipping Firecrawl) are off the table — the math says we don't need them. The achievable optimizations are:

 - Batch Claude calls (5-10 pages per call instead of one per page) → ~50% Claude cost cut on L9

 - Entity-level cache with 30-60 day TTL → 30-60% cost cut on repeat runs

 - Model swap to Haiku ONLY for high-confidence structured pages (staff directories with known format) → ~30% cost cut on easy-path jobs

 - Early termination only when confidence is very high (≥10 verified contacts above 0.9 confidence on an easy-path job) → skips L15-L20 on the easy jobs

 - Job-level parallelism with shared Serper/Exa/Brave token bucket → 3-5x speed up (wall clock), same cost

 These are all structural wins that preserve quality . They should hit the 2-5x cost target without any quality sacrifice. If real-use data later shows the conservative scenario is the truth and we need deeper

 cuts, we revisit then — but not on speculation.

 ---

 All 5 candidates are cold — no district-level match, no school-level accounts underneath. Your territory has:

 ┌─────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ IL │ 21 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ OH │ 9 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ NE │ 4 │

 ├─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤

 │ (all 13 territory states total) │ 176 │

 └─────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

 None of: Cypress-Fairbanks ISD, Cincinnati Public Schools, Conejo Valley USD, Park Ridge-Niles CCSD 64, Waverly School District 145 have any active account at the district level OR at any school underneath

 them (per fuzzy name match at threshold 0.7). So we're running the A/B on 5 fully-cold targets with no Salesforce signal for ground truth. Pure engine-vs-engine comparison plus per-contact quality scoring.

 Caveat: my fuzzy-match threshold is conservative (0.7). If you think any of the 5 SHOULD have been caught (e.g. Cincinnati, which is a major district), tell me and I'll re-run with a looser match. But I don't

 think you have accounts there — the OH total is only 9, which bounds the possibilities.

 ---

 Where that leaves us:

 vs hard" split is a new insight that shapes the design.

 Anything in the yield data or the corrected cost math you want me to rework before I proceed? Otherwise I start step 1 now.

 ❯ Something i am wiling to sacrifice a bit is the speed (for now) i could potenetially schedule some of the seraches for after work hours and overnight.

 ⏺ Good constraint. Absorbed and it simplifies the design significantly.

 ---

 What changes with speed being negotiable:

 Two modes, not one. Engine gets a batch_mode flag:

 ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┬────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Mode │ When it runs │ Wall clock/job │ Parallelism │ Optimizations enabled │

 │ │ │ target │ │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Interactive (default for /research X ) │ On-demand, during │ < 90 sec │ 1 job at a time but layers │ Full depth, no aggressive batching, cache read-through │

 │ │ the day │ │ still parallel within the job │ │

 ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Batch (default for campaigns with >10 │ Scheduled window, │ 5-10 min/job │ 4-way concurrent jobs with │ Full depth + Claude call batching (5-10 pages per call, saves ~50% on L9 cost — only │

 │ entities, OR user schedules overnight) │ 6pm-6am CST │ OK │ shared token bucket │ works when latency doesn't matter) + cache write-through + pre-warm territory_data │

 └───────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Why batching Claude calls is a batch-mode-only win: to batch 10 pages into one Claude call, the engine has to wait for all 10 pages to finish loading before firing the Claude request. Interactively, that's a

 30-60 second wall-clock penalty while you're staring at the progress bar. Overnight, it's free — and it's probably the single biggest cost-cut lever we have on L9 (the Claude extraction layer that's currently

 making one blocking call per page with no batching).

 Scheduling fits Scout's existing cron pattern. Scout already runs morning brief at 9:15am, EOD at 4:30pm, hourly check-ins 10am-4pm CST. Adding "overnight research batch drains the queue from 6pm to 6am" is

 the same pattern. When you kick off a big sweep, Scout says: "Queued 47 districts. Will run tonight starting 6pm CST, estimated cost $7, will report results in tomorrow's morning brief." With an override

 ( /research now <target> ) for jobs you actually need during the day.

 Critically: you're still not sacrificing quality. Both modes run the full 20-layer depth. The only differences are:

 1. Batch mode batches Claude calls (same extractions, just grouped differently in API calls — no change to what's extracted)

 2. Batch mode runs 4 jobs in parallel (same per-job behavior)

 3. Batch mode can pre-warm caches for the region (hitting fewer unique URLs)

 Cost implication: batching Claude alone probably drops per-job cost from $0.35-0.95 into the $0.20-0.55 range. Combined with entity cache hits on repeat runs and model swap on high-confidence pages only, we

 should land in the $0.12-0.30 per-job range — comfortably inside the $25/week budget at the realistic 100 jobs/week saturation level.

 ---

 Now proceeding with my committed next actions. Going to execute and report back, no more questions until done.
