 ⏺ Done reading. Here are the specific wins I found, each with file:line and honest estimates.

 ---

 5 concrete wins I can point at in the code

 Win #1 — L18 and L19 are BOTH running Firecrawl on the same district site (waste)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1095-1266

 L18 ( _layer18_firecrawl_extract ) calls fc.extract(urls=[f"https://{domain}/*"], enable_web_search=True) which crawls the district site AND does a web search, then extracts contacts directly (bypassing Claude).

 L19 ( _layer19_firecrawl_site_map ) separately calls fc.map() + up to 8 fc.scrape() calls on the same district site, then the scraped markdown flows into L9 Claude extraction.

 Both target the same domain with overlapping purpose. L18 and L19 are redundant — either one of them (if it works) covers the staff directory case. Running both means paying Firecrawl twice for the same

 district.

 Plus: L18 has enable_web_search=True set (line 1150), which doubles its cost. Web search is already done by L1/L2/L3/L5/L11/L14/L16/L20 — adding another web search pass inside Firecrawl is redundant.

 Fix: pick ONE. I'd keep L19 (map + scrape → raw markdown → L9 Claude extraction with full context visibility) and disable L18 entirely, OR if L18 stays, set enable_web_search=False .

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.20/job (estimate — Firecrawl's /extract with enable_web_search=True is one of their more expensive operations; exact savings depend on their current pricing which I'd verify before

 shipping). Risk: zero — if L19 alone is insufficient we can flip back.

 ---

 Win #2 — raw_pages is never URL-deduped before L9 Claude extraction

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:_add_raw_from_serper at line 1566, and the raw_pages.append(...) calls scattered through L1-L20.

 Multiple layers can return the SAME URL for the same district:

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 - L11 school-staff search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page (same URL)

 - L16 Exa broad: Austin ISD → possibly same page

 - L4 district-site search: also sometimes

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one

 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Fix: dedupe self.raw_pages by URL before L9 runs. ~5 lines of code at the top of _layer9_claude_extraction :

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which I haven't measured. Could verify with a one-line logger.info before shipping). Risk: zero — removes only

 duplicate work, no quality change.

 ---

 Win #3 — L9 calls Claude Sonnet one page at a time, no batching

 Where: tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 def extract_from_multiple(pages: list [ tuple [ str , str ]], district_name: str ) -> list [ dict ]:

 all_contacts = []

 seen = set ()

 for url, content in pages:

 contacts = extract_contacts(content, url, district_name) # ONE Sonnet call per page

 ...

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,

 that's 20 separate Sonnet calls with identical system prompts.

 Per-call overhead: 400 system tokens × 20 calls = 8,000 tokens of redundant prompt input (~$0.024 wasted on input alone).

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2

 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page

 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test

 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Caveat Steven already flagged: batching adds latency to interactive /research X (wait for all 5 pages before calling Claude) — so this should be batch_mode=True only for the overnight path, and the interactive

 path keeps single-page calls. Gated.

 ---

 Win #4 — L15 Step 5 "discovery" runs unconditionally even when we already have plenty

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1487-1519

 L15's Step 5 does 5 @domain Serper queries to find MORE contacts we might have missed (L1-L14 output), then feeds the results through another extract_from_multiple Claude call. Runs every time, regardless of

 how many VERIFIED contacts L9 already found.

 For "easy" districts where L9 already returned 20+ VERIFIED contacts, Step 5 is searching for more contacts we don't need — marginal yield is typically 0-2 extras.

 Fix: conditional skip. If all_contacts already has ≥10 contacts with email_confidence=VERIFIED (or some threshold you're comfortable with), skip Step 5. Keep Steps 1-4 (those improve existing contacts, not

 just pad the list).

 # ── Step 5: Discovery searches using @domain pattern ─────────────────

 if domain and _l15_used < _L15_MAX and self._serper_count < self._serper_cap:

 verified_count = sum ( 1 for c in self.all_contacts if c.get( 'email_confidence' ) == 'VERIFIED' )

 if verified_count >= 10 :

 logger.info( f"L15 Step 5 skipped: already have {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts" )

 else :

 remaining = min (_L15_MAX - _l15_used, 5 )

 ...

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1-2 Claude batches per "easy" job = ~$0.02-0.05/job on easy targets only (estimate — only applies when the threshold triggers). Risk: very low — you explicitly set the

 "we have enough" threshold.

 ---

 Win #5 — _merge_contacts silently drops higher-quality duplicates (THIS IS A QUALITY BUG, not a cost bug)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 AND tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 2 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, john.smith@district.edu, VERIFIED" — the page 2 record is DROPPED because John Smith is already in the seen set from page 1.

 This is a silent quality loss. Real scenario: L1 staff directory page has names but no emails. L11 school-staff page has the same name WITH email. Current code keeps the no-email version from L1.

 Fix: upgrade-on-merge logic. If a new contact has the same name as an existing one but has BETTER data (email when existing had none, VERIFIED when existing was INFERRED, etc.), REPLACE the existing record.

 def _merge_contacts(self, new_contacts: list [ dict ]):

 existing_by_key = {}

 for c in self.all_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 conf_rank = { "VERIFIED" : 4 , "LIKELY" : 3 , "INFERRED" : 2 , "UNKNOWN" : 1 , "" : 0 }

 for c in new_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 if key not in existing_by_key:

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 self.all_contacts.append(c)

 else :

 old = existing_by_key[key]

 old_conf = conf_rank.get(old.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 new_conf = conf_rank.get(c.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 # Upgrade if new has email and old didn't, or new has better confidence

 if (c.get( 'email' ) and not old.get( 'email' )) or new_conf > old_conf:

 old.update({k: v for k, v in c.items() if v})

 Estimated impact: ZERO cost savings, but non-trivial quality gain — potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Worth doing because "as good as possible" is your bar.

 Risk: very low — upgrading is strictly better than dropping.

 ---

 My recommended order to ship

 1. Win #2 (URL dedup) — tiny diff, zero risk, measurable savings immediately. ~30 min work.

 2. Win #5 (merge-upgrade) — tiny diff, pure quality gain. ~45 min work.

 3. Win #1 (L18 disable or enable_web_search=False ) — one-line change, measurable savings. ~15 min work.

 4. Win #4 (L15 Step 5 skip-when-enough) — ~10 line conditional. ~30 min work.

 5. Win #3 (L9 batching) — biggest cost win but requires careful rewrite + validation. ~half day work, gated to batch mode only.

 Wins 1-4 together: ~2 hours of work, estimated ~$0.15-0.40/job savings, zero quality risk, plus recovers some lost VERIFIED emails.

 Win 3 alone: ~half day work, estimated additional ~$0.10-0.30/job savings, low risk with A/B validation, batch-mode only.

 ---

 My proposal: start with Wins #1, #2, #4, and #5 right now as separate small commits. They're surgical, low-risk, and we can measure the combined effect by running one of the 5 test targets through both engines

 (old commit vs new commit on the same target).

 Then we evaluate, and if the numbers look good, we take on Win #3 as a slightly bigger piece of work.

 Do you want me to start building Wins #1/#2/#4/#5 as 4 small commits right now? Or adjust the order / add / remove anything first?

 ⏺ Huge finding from Research Log notes field. The per-layer contact yield data is stored there and I just got a first look at real production numbers:

 ┌──────────────────────┬──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ District │ Size │ Top yielding layers (contacts/layer) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Houston ISD │ 200k │ L16 Exa-broad: 39 / L17 Exa-domain: 17 / L15: 10 / L1-L3: 12 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Columbus City │ 45k │ L16 Exa-broad: 58 / L17 Exa-domain: 15 / L13 state-doe: 5 / L1-L3: 6 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Leander ISD │ 40k │ L15: 13 / L1 direct: 5 / L2 variations: 5 / L16: 3 / L3-L5-L14: 4 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Guthrie Public │ ~2k │ L16: 25 / L15: 10 / L13: 9 / L2: 3 / L17: 5 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Desert Sands │ 27k │ L2 variations: 13 / L3 LinkedIn: 12 / L1: 4 / L14: 4 / L5: 3 / L15: 1 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Irving STEAM │ ~2k │ L2: 10 / L14: 3 / L4: 1 (everything else: 0) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Lackland ISD (retry) │ 7k │ L16: 18 / L2: 5 / L14: 2 / L15: 2 │

 └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit
 rate but dominates when it hits (mean 28.6 contacts). L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low hit rate (10-35%) and low mean yield — dead weight candidates. And territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment is failing on 10
 of 20 job because the state field in the Research Log is truncated ("Te", "Oh", "Ok" instead of "TX", "OH", "OK") — that's a data quality issue in the log, not an engine problem, but it means a size-based
 tier syst m would misfir .

 ❯ 1. good with that. 2. explain i dont understand what your asking

 ⏺ No problem. Q4 was jargon. Let me rewrite it in plain language and make it concrete.

 ---

 Q4 in plain English: the new research engine isn't going to be one big rewrite. It's going to be a list of 6-8 specific improvements , each one a separate piece of code I can build, test, and measure on its

 own. The question I'm asking is: which of those improvements do we build into the FIRST version of the new engine, and which do we leave for later?

 Why this matters: if I build all 8 at once, and the A/B test shows the new engine is 5% worse at finding contacts, we won't know WHICH change caused it. If I build them separately and turn them on one at a

 time, we can measure each one cleanly. But building fewer at once means a smaller cost win in round 1. There's a tradeoff and you get to pick where the line is.

 Here's the full menu with my read on each. Plain English, honest estimates labeled by provenance, my recommendation at the end of each row.

 ---

 The 8 possible improvements:

 1. Remember results for 30-60 days (entity cache).

 - What: After researching Cypress-Fairbanks ISD once, Scout remembers the contacts for 30-60 days. Research the same district again → instant, zero cost. You can force a fresh run with a --fresh flag.

 - Cost savings: big when you re-research anything (estimate: 30-60% of calls become free over time, depends on how much your targets overlap week-to-week)

 - Quality risk: none — you can always force fresh

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 2. Bundle Claude calls (batch extraction).

 - What: Today, the engine sends 10 separate requests to Claude for 10 web pages. The new version bundles all 10 pages into 1 request. Same contacts extracted, ~50% fewer API calls.

 - Cost savings: cuts the biggest single cost driver roughly in half (measurement: L9 Claude extraction is currently 40-60% of total per-job cost, this cuts it ~50%)

 - Quality risk: minimal if built carefully. Claude handles multi-doc inputs well.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (rewrite extract_from_multiple in tools/contact_extractor.py , A/B test against current golden outputs)

 - Catch: only works in batch mode / overnight runs — for interactive /research X requests, bundling means waiting for all 10 pages to load before firing Claude, which adds 30-60 seconds of wall clock.

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode

 3. Use Haiku (cheap model) on easy pages only.

 - What: When a page is clearly a structured staff directory (name/title/email in a table or list), use Claude Haiku 4.5 — ~10x cheaper. When a page is ambiguous (news article, board meeting minutes), keep

 Sonnet.

 - Cost savings: 20-30% on the Claude extraction cost, depends on what fraction of pages qualify as "easy structured" (unknown until we measure)

 - Quality risk: LOW if the page classifier is accurate. HIGHER if the classifier mislabels ambiguous pages as easy. Needs an A/B validation before it ships as default.

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (classifier + model swap + validation)

 - My pick: BUILD but default OFF. Turn on later after the A/B proves it's safe. Measuring it in round 1 tells us whether it's worth shipping.

 4. Run 4 jobs at the same time (parallelism).

 - What: Today, ResearchQueue runs one job at a time to avoid Serper rate limits. The new version runs up to 4 jobs concurrently, using a shared rate-limit budget so they don't step on each other.

 - Cost savings: 0% (same total work, same API calls)

 - Speed savings: 3-4x wall clock on campaigns (a 50-district sweep goes from 4 hours serial to ~1 hour parallel)

 - Quality risk: minimal — if rate limits trip, jobs retry automatically

 - Build effort: ~2-3 days (token bucket + worker pool + retry logic)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, ON in batch mode, OFF in interactive mode (interactive runs 1 job at a time by definition — you typed one command)

 5. Stop early when the engine already has enough.

 - What: If by layer 6 the engine has already found 10+ verified contacts with 0.9+ confidence on an easy target, skip layers 7-20. Current engine runs all 20 layers regardless.

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy jobs

 - Quality risk: MEDIUM — this is the one that could actually miss the long-tail contacts that only appear in L12-L20. Needs careful threshold tuning against A/B results before it's safe to default on.

 - Build effort: ~1 day (threshold check after each phase)

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Don't build it until we have real A/B data showing the threshold is safe. Otherwise we're cutting quality on speculation.

 6. Remember district domains (territory list as a shortcut).

 - What: Layer 4 today does ~6-8 Serper queries to discover a district's website domain (e.g., "cfisd.net" for Cypress-Fairbanks). The new version checks if we already discovered that domain in a previous run —

 if yes, skip L4 entirely.

 - Cost savings: 10-20% on any repeat district, 5-10% amortized

 - Quality risk: none (fall back to L4 if we don't have a cached domain)

 - Build effort: ~1 day (add a column to territory_data or sibling cache, lookup in L4)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON

 7. Detect "easy" vs "hard" districts and route differently.

 - What: After the first 3 layers, if we've found the district's staff directory URL, run a shorter fast-path (skip redundant search layers). If we haven't, run the full depth. This is what I noticed in the

 Research Log yield data — some districts return 80+ emails (easy), some return 0-5 (hard).

 - Cost savings: 20-40% on easy targets, 0% on hard ones

 - Quality risk: low if the "easy" detector is conservative. Ship the detector as "only take fast path when VERY confident we have the directory."

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days

 - My pick: SKIP in round 1. Nice-to-have but not essential. Revisit after we see what % of targets are truly "easy" in the A/B data.

 8. Overnight batch scheduler.

 - What: When you kick off a campaign ( /sweep all districts in IL over 20k ), Scout says "queued 23 districts, running tonight 6pm-6am CST, results in tomorrow's morning brief." You get a one-line confirmation

 now and results in the morning. Small campaigns still run immediately if you want.

 - Cost savings: 0% direct, but this is what UNLOCKS improvement #2 (Claude batching) for campaign runs — batching only makes sense when latency is free, which it is overnight

 - Quality risk: none — just UX

 - Build effort: ~1-2 days (scheduler hook + campaign shape detection + morning brief integration)

 - My pick: SHIP in round 1, default ON for campaigns with >10 entities

 ---

 My recommended round 1 lineup:

 Ship these five:

 - #1 Cache (big win, no risk)

 - #2 Claude batching (biggest single cost cut, batch mode only)

 - #4 Job parallelism (campaigns go from hours to minutes)

 - #6 Territory domain shortcut (free win)

 - #8 Batch scheduler (unlocks #2 for campaigns)

 Build but default OFF:

 - #3 Haiku on easy pages (measure it in A/B, ship later if it passes)

 Skip for now:

 - #5 Early termination (too much quality risk without A/B data first)

 - #7 Easy/hard routing (nice-to-have, revisit later)

 Estimated round 1 impact (derived from the savings estimates above, labeled estimate — not measurement ):

 - Per-job cost: $0.35-0.95 today → $0.15-0.45 after round 1 (~2x reduction, lower end of the 2-5x target)

 - Wall clock for a 50-district campaign: ~4 hours serial → ~50 min parallel + batched

 - Interactive /research X latency: unchanged (still 3-8 min, because batching and parallelism don't apply to single-target runs)

 Round 1 build time estimate: ~6-10 working days of plan-mode + implementation + A/B harness build.

 ---

 Q4 restated in one sentence: Do you approve this round 1 lineup, or do you want to swap something in/out?

 Options:

 - (a) Approve as-is. I build the 5 ship items + #3 toggle, skip #5 and #7, and we run the A/B test after round 1 is done.

 - (b) Approve with modifications. Tell me which items you want added (e.g., "add #5, I'll take the risk") or removed (e.g., "don't bother with #6, too small a win for the complexity").

 - (c) Something's unclear. Tell me which one doesn't make sense and I'll explain it more.

 ❯ OK i think this method your seuggesting will take too long and be too many steps. we just need to start trying to figure things out. We should do our absolute best to examine everything we have look at

 everything carefully and creatively implement improvements and test as we go. We can do a really long in depth fix another time.

 ⏺ Heard. Reframing: skip the 8-item menu approach, skip the giant plan doc for later, actually dig into the code now and find specific wins I can point at.

 Here's what I'm about to do (should take ~15 min):

 1. Read tools/research_engine.py (all 1,795 lines) and tools/contact_extractor.py end-to-end — not skim, read. Look for actual inefficiencies, dead code, redundant queries, bad model choices, anything that's

 wasting money or time that I can point at with a file:line.

 2. Pull the Research Log notes field for the 20 public-district jobs I already found — see if layer-by-layer stats are in there, and if so which layers actually produce contacts vs which run and find nothing.

 3. Come back with 3-5 concrete improvements I've seen in the code , each with: what I saw, which file:line, why it's wasteful, what I'd change, and how risky the change is.

 Then we pick 1-2 to ship as quick wins, I build them, we A/B test against the current engine on one of the 5 targets, measure, iterate. Each win is a separate small commit, not a bundled rewrite.

 No more menu-style questions. Starting now.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Done reading. Here are the specific wins I found, each with file:line and honest estimates.

 ---

 5 concrete wins I can point at in the code

 Win #1 — L18 and L19 are BOTH running Firecrawl on the same district site (waste)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1095-1266

 L18 ( _layer18_firecrawl_extract ) calls fc.extract(urls=[f"https://{domain}/*"], enable_web_search=True) which crawls the district site AND does a web search, then extracts contacts directly (bypassing Claude).

 L19 ( _layer19_firecrawl_site_map ) separately calls fc.map() + up to 8 fc.scrape() calls on the same district site, then the scraped markdown flows into L9 Claude extraction.

 Both target the same domain with overlapping purpose. L18 and L19 are redundant — either one of them (if it works) covers the staff directory case. Running both means paying Firecrawl twice for the same

 district.

 Plus: L18 has enable_web_search=True set (line 1150), which doubles its cost. Web search is already done by L1/L2/L3/L5/L11/L14/L16/L20 — adding another web search pass inside Firecrawl is redundant.

 Fix: pick ONE. I'd keep L19 (map + scrape → raw markdown → L9 Claude extraction with full context visibility) and disable L18 entirely, OR if L18 stays, set enable_web_search=False .

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.20/job (estimate — Firecrawl's /extract with enable_web_search=True is one of their more expensive operations; exact savings depend on their current pricing which I'd verify before

 shipping). Risk: zero — if L19 alone is insufficient we can flip back.

 ---

 Win #2 — raw_pages is never URL-deduped before L9 Claude extraction

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:_add_raw_from_serper at line 1566, and the raw_pages.append(...) calls scattered through L1-L20.

 Multiple layers can return the SAME URL for the same district:

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 - L11 school-staff search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page (same URL)

 - L16 Exa broad: Austin ISD → possibly same page

 - L4 district-site search: also sometimes

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one

 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Fix: dedupe self.raw_pages by URL before L9 runs. ~5 lines of code at the top of _layer9_claude_extraction :

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which I haven't measured. Could verify with a one-line logger.info before shipping). Risk: zero — removes only

 duplicate work, no quality change.

 ---

 Win #3 — L9 calls Claude Sonnet one page at a time, no batching

 Where: tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 def extract_from_multiple(pages: list [ tuple [ str , str ]], district_name: str ) -> list [ dict ]:

 all_contacts = []

 seen = set ()

 for url, content in pages:

 contacts = extract_contacts(content, url, district_name) # ONE Sonnet call per page

 ...

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,

 that's 20 separate Sonnet calls with identical system prompts.

 Per-call overhead: 400 system tokens × 20 calls = 8,000 tokens of redundant prompt input (~$0.024 wasted on input alone).

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2

 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page

 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test

 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Caveat Steven already flagged: batching adds latency to interactive /research X (wait for all 5 pages before calling Claude) — so this should be batch_mode=True only for the overnight path, and the interactive

 path keeps single-page calls. Gated.

 ---

 Win #4 — L15 Step 5 "discovery" runs unconditionally even when we already have plenty

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1487-1519

 L15's Step 5 does 5 @domain Serper queries to find MORE contacts we might have missed (L1-L14 output), then feeds the results through another extract_from_multiple Claude call. Runs every time, regardless of

 how many VERIFIED contacts L9 already found.

 For "easy" districts where L9 already returned 20+ VERIFIED contacts, Step 5 is searching for more contacts we don't need — marginal yield is typically 0-2 extras.

 Fix: conditional skip. If all_contacts already has ≥10 contacts with email_confidence=VERIFIED (or some threshold you're comfortable with), skip Step 5. Keep Steps 1-4 (those improve existing contacts, not

 just pad the list).

 # ── Step 5: Discovery searches using @domain pattern ─────────────────

 if domain and _l15_used < _L15_MAX and self._serper_count < self._serper_cap:

 verified_count = sum ( 1 for c in self.all_contacts if c.get( 'email_confidence' ) == 'VERIFIED' )

 if verified_count >= 10 :

 logger.info( f"L15 Step 5 skipped: already have {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts" )

 else :

 remaining = min (_L15_MAX - _l15_used, 5 )

 ...

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1-2 Claude batches per "easy" job = ~$0.02-0.05/job on easy targets only (estimate — only applies when the threshold triggers). Risk: very low — you explicitly set the

 "we have enough" threshold.

 ---

 Win #5 — _merge_contacts silently drops higher-quality duplicates (THIS IS A QUALITY BUG, not a cost bug)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 AND tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 2 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, john.smith@district.edu, VERIFIED" — the page 2 record is DROPPED because John Smith is already in the seen set from page 1.

 This is a silent quality loss. Real scenario: L1 staff directory page has names but no emails. L11 school-staff page has the same name WITH email. Current code keeps the no-email version from L1.

 Fix: upgrade-on-merge logic. If a new contact has the same name as an existing one but has BETTER data (email when existing had none, VERIFIED when existing was INFERRED, etc.), REPLACE the existing record.

 def _merge_contacts(self, new_contacts: list [ dict ]):

 existing_by_key = {}

 for c in self.all_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 conf_rank = { "VERIFIED" : 4 , "LIKELY" : 3 , "INFERRED" : 2 , "UNKNOWN" : 1 , "" : 0 }

 for c in new_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 if key not in existing_by_key:

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 self.all_contacts.append(c)

 else :

 old = existing_by_key[key]

 old_conf = conf_rank.get(old.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 new_conf = conf_rank.get(c.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 # Upgrade if new has email and old didn't, or new has better confidence

 if (c.get( 'email' ) and not old.get( 'email' )) or new_conf > old_conf:

 old.update({k: v for k, v in c.items() if v})

 Estimated impact: ZERO cost savings, but non-trivial quality gain — potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Worth doing because "as good as possible" is your bar.

 Risk: very low — upgrading is strictly better than dropping.

 ---

 My recommended order to ship

 1. Win #2 (URL dedup) — tiny diff, zero risk, measurable savings immediately. ~30 min work.

 2. Win #5 (merge-upgrade) — tiny diff, pure quality gain. ~45 min work.

 3. Win #1 (L18 disable or enable_web_search=False ) — one-line change, measurable savings. ~15 min work.

 4. Win #4 (L15 Step 5 skip-when-enough) — ~10 line conditional. ~30 min work.

 5. Win #3 (L9 batching) — biggest cost win but requires careful rewrite + validation. ~half day work, gated to batch mode only.

 Wins 1-4 together: ~2 hours of work, estimated ~$0.15-0.40/job savings, zero quality risk, plus recovers some lost VERIFIED emails.

 Win 3 alone: ~half day work, estimated additional ~$0.10-0.30/job savings, low risk with A/B validation, batch-mode only.

 ---

 My proposal: start with Wins #1, #2, #4, and #5 right now as separate small commits. They're surgical, low-risk, and we can measure the combined effect by running one of the 5 test targets through both engines

 (old commit vs new commit on the same target).

 Then we evaluate, and if the numbers look good, we take on Win #3 as a slightly bigger piece of work.

 Do you want me to start building Wins #1/#2/#4/#5 as 4 small commits right now? Or adjust the order / add / remove anything first?

 not a current cost.

 ⏺ Huge finding from Research Log notes field. The per-layer contact yield data is stored there and I just got a first look at real production numbers:

 ┌──────────────────────┬──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ District │ Size │ Top yielding layers (contacts/layer) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Houston ISD │ 200k │ L16 Exa-broad: 39 / L17 Exa-domain: 17 / L15: 10 / L1-L3: 12 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Columbus City │ 45k │ L16 Exa-broad: 58 / L17 Exa-domain: 15 / L13 state-doe: 5 / L1-L3: 6 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Leander ISD │ 40k │ L15: 13 / L1 direct: 5 / L2 variations: 5 / L16: 3 / L3-L5-L14: 4 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Guthrie Public │ ~2k │ L16: 25 / L15: 10 / L13: 9 / L2: 3 / L17: 5 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Desert Sands │ 27k │ L2 variations: 13 / L3 LinkedIn: 12 / L1: 4 / L14: 4 / L5: 3 / L15: 1 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Irving STEAM │ ~2k │ L2: 10 / L14: 3 / L4: 1 (everything else: 0) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Lackland ISD (retry) │ 7k │ L16: 18 / L2: 5 / L14: 2 / L15: 2 │

 └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit

 rate but dominates when it hits (mean 28.6 contacts). L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low hit rate (10-35%) and low mean yield — dead weight candidates. And territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment is failing on 10

 of 20 jobs because the state field in the Research Log is truncated ("Te", "Oh", "Ok" instead of "TX", "OH", "OK") — that's a data quality issue in the log, not an engine problem, but it means a size-based

 tier system would misfire.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Done reading. Here are the specific wins I found, each with file:line and honest estimates.

 ---

 5 concrete wins I can point at in the code

 Win #1 — L18 and L19 are BOTH running Firecrawl on the same district site (waste)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1095-1266

 L18 ( _layer18_firecrawl_extract ) calls fc.extract(urls=[f"https://{domain}/*"], enable_web_search=True) which crawls the district site AND does a web search, then extracts contacts directly (bypassing Claude).

 L19 ( _layer19_firecrawl_site_map ) separately calls fc.map() + up to 8 fc.scrape() calls on the same district site, then the scraped markdown flows into L9 Claude extraction.

 Both target the same domain with overlapping purpose. L18 and L19 are redundant — either one of them (if it works) covers the staff directory case. Running both means paying Firecrawl twice for the same

 district.

 Plus: L18 has enable_web_search=True set (line 1150), which doubles its cost. Web search is already done by L1/L2/L3/L5/L11/L14/L16/L20 — adding another web search pass inside Firecrawl is redundant.

 Fix: pick ONE. I'd keep L19 (map + scrape → raw markdown → L9 Claude extraction with full context visibility) and disable L18 entirely, OR if L18 stays, set enable_web_search=False .

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.20/job (estimate — Firecrawl's /extract with enable_web_search=True is one of their more expensive operations; exact savings depend on their current pricing which I'd verify before

 shipping). Risk: zero — if L19 alone is insufficient we can flip back.

 ---

 Win #2 — raw_pages is never URL-deduped before L9 Claude extraction

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:_add_raw_from_serper at line 1566, and the raw_pages.append(...) calls scattered through L1-L20.

 Multiple layers can return the SAME URL for the same district:

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 - L11 school-staff search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page (same URL)

 - L16 Exa broad: Austin ISD → possibly same page

 - L4 district-site search: also sometimes

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one

 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Fix: dedupe self.raw_pages by URL before L9 runs. ~5 lines of code at the top of _layer9_claude_extraction :

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which I haven't measured. Could verify with a one-line logger.info before shipping). Risk: zero — removes only

 duplicate work, no quality change.

 ---

 Win #3 — L9 calls Claude Sonnet one page at a time, no batching

 Where: tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 def extract_from_multiple(pages: list [ tuple [ str , str ]], district_name: str ) -> list [ dict ]:

 all_contacts = []

 seen = set ()

 for url, content in pages:

 contacts = extract_contacts(content, url, district_name) # ONE Sonnet call per page

 ...

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,

 that's 20 separate Sonnet calls with identical system prompts.

 Per-call overhead: 400 system tokens × 20 calls = 8,000 tokens of redundant prompt input (~$0.024 wasted on input alone).

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2

 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page

 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test

 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Caveat Steven already flagged: batching adds latency to interactive /research X (wait for all 5 pages before calling Claude) — so this should be batch_mode=True only for the overnight path, and the interactive

 path keeps single-page calls. Gated.

 ---

 Win #4 — L15 Step 5 "discovery" runs unconditionally even when we already have plenty

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1487-1519

 L15's Step 5 does 5 @domain Serper queries to find MORE contacts we might have missed (L1-L14 output), then feeds the results through another extract_from_multiple Claude call. Runs every time, regardless of

 how many VERIFIED contacts L9 already found.

 For "easy" districts where L9 already returned 20+ VERIFIED contacts, Step 5 is searching for more contacts we don't need — marginal yield is typically 0-2 extras.

 Fix: conditional skip. If all_contacts already has ≥10 contacts with email_confidence=VERIFIED (or some threshold you're comfortable with), skip Step 5. Keep Steps 1-4 (those improve existing contacts, not

 just pad the list).

 # ── Step 5: Discovery searches using @domain pattern ─────────────────

 if domain and _l15_used < _L15_MAX and self._serper_count < self._serper_cap:

 verified_count = sum ( 1 for c in self.all_contacts if c.get( 'email_confidence' ) == 'VERIFIED' )

 if verified_count >= 10 :

 logger.info( f"L15 Step 5 skipped: already have {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts" )

 else :

 remaining = min (_L15_MAX - _l15_used, 5 )

 ...

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1-2 Claude batches per "easy" job = ~$0.02-0.05/job on easy targets only (estimate — only applies when the threshold triggers). Risk: very low — you explicitly set the

 "we have enough" threshold.

 ---

 Win #5 — _merge_contacts silently drops higher-quality duplicates (THIS IS A QUALITY BUG, not a cost bug)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 AND tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 2 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, john.smith@district.edu, VERIFIED" — the page 2 record is DROPPED because John Smith is already in the seen set from page 1.

 This is a silent quality loss. Real scenario: L1 staff directory page has names but no emails. L11 school-staff page has the same name WITH email. Current code keeps the no-email version from L1.

 Fix: upgrade-on-merge logic. If a new contact has the same name as an existing one but has BETTER data (email when existing had none, VERIFIED when existing was INFERRED, etc.), REPLACE the existing record.

 def _merge_contacts(self, new_contacts: list [ dict ]):

 existing_by_key = {}

 for c in self.all_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 conf_rank = { "VERIFIED" : 4 , "LIKELY" : 3 , "INFERRED" : 2 , "UNKNOWN" : 1 , "" : 0 }

 for c in new_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 if key not in existing_by_key:

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 self.all_contacts.append(c)

 else :

 old = existing_by_key[key]

 old_conf = conf_rank.get(old.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 new_conf = conf_rank.get(c.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 # Upgrade if new has email and old didn't, or new has better confidence

 if (c.get( 'email' ) and not old.get( 'email' )) or new_conf > old_conf:

 old.update({k: v for k, v in c.items() if v})

 Estimated impact: ZERO cost savings, but non-trivial quality gain — potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Worth doing because "as good as possible" is your bar.

 Risk: very low — upgrading is strictly better than dropping.

 ---

 My recommended order to ship

 1. Win #2 (URL dedup) — tiny diff, zero risk, measurable savings immediately. ~30 min work.

 2. Win #5 (merge-upgrade) — tiny diff, pure quality gain. ~45 min work.

 3. Win #1 (L18 disable or enable_web_search=False ) — one-line change, measurable savings. ~15 min work.

 4. Win #4 (L15 Step 5 skip-when-enough) — ~10 line conditional. ~30 min work.

 5. Win #3 (L9 batching) — biggest cost win but requires careful rewrite + validation. ~half day work, gated to batch mode only.

 Wins 1-4 together: ~2 hours of work, estimated ~$0.15-0.40/job savings, zero quality risk, plus recovers some lost VERIFIED emails.

 Win 3 alone: ~half day work, estimated additional ~$0.10-0.30/job savings, low risk with A/B validation, batch-mode only.

 ---

 My proposal: start with Wins #1, #2, #4, and #5 right now as separate small commits. They're surgical, low-risk, and we can measure the combined effect by running one of the 5 test targets through both engines

 (old commit vs new commit on the same target).

 Then we evaluate, and if the numbers look good, we take on Win #3 as a slightly bigger piece of work.

 Do you want me to start building Wins #1/#2/#4/#5 as 4 small commits right now? Or adjust the order / add / remove anything first?

 ❯ ok, just fyi- i havent paid for firecrawl still. that is just a placeholder and is supposed to be gracefully skipped for now. I want you to enter plan mode and really look carefully at everything and all the

 different posible combinations and configurations and settings for what we currently have and how we could improve upon it.

 ⏺ Good catch on Firecrawl — retracting Win #1 since L18/L19 gracefully skip when FIRECRAWL_API_KEY isn't set. Verified both fail-safe at research_engine.py:1109-1112 and 1209-1211 . That's a landmine for future,

 not a current cost.

 Entering deep exploration mode. I'll look at every configurable thing (env vars, constants, thresholds, model choices, layer query counts, rate limits, caps, filter thresholds), map all tunable dimensions,

 check Research Log notes for real per-job signals, enumerate the combinations that actually make sense, and come back with a plan. Loading Enter Plan Mode in parallel so it's ready.

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 I have enough to write the plan. Entering plan mode.

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌

 Research Engine Round 1 — Surgical Wins + A/B Harness

 Session: 60

 Author: Claude (Steven's AI sales partner)

 Plan file: ~/.claude/plans/spicy-sleeping-gadget.md

 ---

 Context

 Scout's research engine ( tools/research_engine.py , 1,795 lines, 20-layer pipeline) is the bottleneck for bulk prospecting work. Current per-job economics are too expensive for Steven's real-use budget:

 - Wall clock: mean 6-7 min/job serial

 - Serper queries: ~57/job

 - Cost: ~$0.35-0.95/job (estimate from Serper + Exa + Brave + ~10-30 Claude Sonnet 4.6 calls)

 - Queue concurrency: 1 job at a time (serial by design, ResearchQueue singleton)

 Steven's real constraints (from Session 60 conversation):

 - Budget: $25/week maximum , lower is better

 - Email cap: Outreach 5,000/rolling-7-days per user → ~100 research jobs/week at saturation if avg yield holds

 - Quality bar: no sacrifice — every optimization must preserve or improve contact yield

 - Speed: negotiable — batch/overnight scheduling is acceptable for bulk work

 - Primary lane: public school districts in 13 territory states (TX, CA-SoCal, IL, PA, OH, MI, CT, OK, MA, IN, NV, TN, NE). Diocesan, charter CMOs, CTE centers, private networks are secondary lanes and do NOT

 lead the design.

 - Preserve old engine for A/B testing before retiring it

 Goal of this plan: ship a surgical, low-risk, measurable Round 1 set of improvements to a new engine ( research_engine_v2 ) running alongside the existing engine ( research_engine.py ) and prove the wins via a

 purpose-built A/B test harness before flipping any defaults.

 ---

 Measurement (the real data this plan is anchored to)

 Pulled from the Research Log tab of Steven's main Google Sheet (N=20 public district jobs, diocesan/charter/CTE/private excluded). Per-layer yield data parsed from the notes field.

 Per-layer contact yield aggregates:

 ┌─────────────────────┬──────────┬───────────────────────────┬────────┬───────┬─────┐

 │ Layer │ Hit rate │ Mean yield (when present) │ Median │ Total │ Max │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L1 direct-title │ 75% │ 10.4 │ 9 │ 156 │ 24 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L2 title-variations │ 90% │ 12.6 │ 14 │ 227 │ 30 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L3 linkedin │ 75% │ 7.7 │ 7 │ 115 │ 14 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L4 district-site │ 35% │ 1.4 │ 1 │ 10 │ 3 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L5 news-grants │ 25% │ 1.6 │ 1 │ 8 │ 3 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L11 school-staff │ 20% │ 2.0 │ 2 │ 8 │ 4 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L12 board-agendas │ 20% │ 1.0 │ 1 │ 4 │ 1 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L13 state-doe │ 15% │ 5.0 │ 5 │ 15 │ 9 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L14 conference │ 70% │ 3.8 │ 3 │ 53 │ 7 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L15 email-verify │ 40% │ 6.0 │ 5 │ 48 │ 13 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L16 exa-broad │ 25% │ 28.6 │ 25 │ 143 │ 58 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L17 exa-domain │ 20% │ 9.5 │ 15 │ 38 │ 17 │

 ├─────────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────┼────────┼───────┼─────┤

 │ L20 brave │ 10% │ 3.0 │ 5 │ 6 │ 5 │

 └─────────────────────┴──────────┴───────────────────────────┴────────┴───────┴─────┘

 Key findings:

 - L1, L2, L3 are the reliable core — 75-90% hit rate, stable mean yield 7-13 contacts/job each. Together they account for 498 of the ~900 total contacts observed. Do not cut these.

 - L2 is the single biggest workhorse — 90% hit rate, mean 12.6, reaches 30 on good runs. Highest-confidence layer.

 - L14 (conference presenter search) is quietly reliable — 70% hit rate. Kept in the core.

 - L16 (Exa broad) is high-variance — only 25% hit rate but mean 28.6 when it hits, max 58 (Columbus City Schools). When it works it dominates the run. When it doesn't, it adds cost without yield.

 - L17 (Exa domain) mirrors L16 — 20% hit rate, mean 9.5 when it hits. Also high variance.

 - L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low-yield candidates — 10-35% hit rate, mean 1-5 contacts. Candidates for conditional execution (not default-on) OR pruning in later rounds if yield stays low.

 - L4 is low direct yield but high indirect value — it discovers district_domain which feeds L6/L7/L11/L17. Do not prune L4 without a replacement.

 L16/L17 cost amplification (key insight for why they're expensive):

 - L16 fires 2 Exa queries → 10-20 pages added to raw_pages

 - L17 fires 2 more Exa queries → another 10-20 pages added to raw_pages

 - All those pages flow into L9 Claude Sonnet 4.6 extraction at ~$0.045/page

 - So the indirect L9 cost of L16/L17 is ~$0.45-0.90/job when they run, far exceeding the ~$0.04 direct Exa cost

 - This is why cutting L16/L17 when they won't yield is the single biggest cost lever

 Enrollment-based tier is unreliable:

 - territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment failed on 10 of 20 jobs because the Research Log stores the state field as "Te" / "Oh" / "Ok" (truncated) instead of the 2-letter abbreviations the function expects

 - This is a data quality issue in the log writer, not a territory_data bug

 - But it means a plain "skip L16/L17 if enrollment < 10k" rule can't be reliably implemented today

 - Implication: the tier must be yield-based, not size-based

 The 2 silent quality bugs in dedup logic:

 - _merge_contacts at tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 dedupes by (first_name, last_name) and drops the newer contact silently. If page 1 returns "John Smith, no email" and page 2 returns "John Smith,

 john@district.edu, VERIFIED", page 2 is dropped — the email is lost.

 - extract_from_multiple at tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235 has the identical bug.

 - Confirmed by reading code. Exists in both engines equally. Affects quality today.

 ---

 Round 1 proposed changes

 Narrow scope, surgical, no rewrites, everything measurable. Each part ships as a separate commit.

 Part 0 — Shared dedup fix (affects both engines)

 Files to modify:

 - tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 — ResearchJob._merge_contacts

 - tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235 — extract_from_multiple

 Change: replace "drop on name collision" logic with "upgrade existing on name collision." When a new contact has the same (first_name, last_name) as an existing contact in the merge set:

 - If new has an email and existing didn't → copy the email to existing

 - If new has higher email_confidence (VERIFIED > LIKELY > INFERRED > UNKNOWN) → upgrade existing's confidence and source_url

 - If new has a title and existing had empty title → copy title

 - Otherwise → leave existing alone

 - Never drop the new contact silently and never downgrade existing.

 Why this ships as Part 0 (not as a v2-only feature): it's a strict quality improvement, not a redesign. Both engines benefit equally. The A/B test's baseline (v1) gets the fix too, which means the A/B

 measurement of intrinsic v2 wins isn't polluted by "v2 is better because it has the dedup fix."

 - _merge_contacts at 4 sites: L9 tools/research_engine.py:729 , L15 Step 4 at 1479, L15 Step 5 at 1517, L18 at 1182 (latter skips gracefully without Firecrawl key)

 - extract_from_multiple at 3 sites: L9 at 711, L15 Step 4 at 1477, L15 Step 5 at 1515

 Risk: very low. Upgrade-on-collision is strictly better than drop-on-collision; there's no scenario where the current behavior is intentionally correct.

 Estimated impact: quality gain, potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Zero cost change.

 ---

 Part 1 — research_engine_v2 alongside research_engine

 File to create: tools/research_engine_v2.py (new, ~200-300 lines)

 Design pattern: Research JobV2(Research Job) subclass inheriting from tools/research_engine.py:Research Job . Override only what changes. All BUG 4/5 fixes, diocesan playbook, contamination filter, and helper

 methods stay intact by inheritance.

 Three changes in v2:

 (A) URL dedup before L9

 Current bug: raw_pages is a flat list appended to by every layer. L1 might return a staff directory URL, L11 might return the same URL, L16 might return it again. All three end up in raw_pages as separate

 entries. L9's 2-pass content filter does NOT dedupe by URL — so the same URL gets sent to Claude 2-3 times in one job.

 Fix: add 5 lines to the top of _layer9_claude_extraction override:

 async def _layer9_claude_extraction(self):

 # v2: dedupe raw_pages by URL before extraction

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 before = len (self.raw_pages)

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 if before > len (deduped):

 logger.info( f"v2 L9 URL dedup: {before - len (deduped)} duplicate URLs removed" )

 # Call parent L9 logic

 return await super ()._layer9_claude_extraction()

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which varies). Risk: zero. Removes pure duplicate work.

 (B) L15 Step 5 conditional skip when yield is already sufficient

 Current: _layer15_email_verification Step 5 runs unconditionally, firing up to 5 @domain discovery queries + another extract_from_multiple Claude batch. Marginal value is 0-2 extra contacts when the engine has

 already found 20+ VERIFIED contacts.

 Fix: override _layer15_email_verification . Before Step 5 (line 1487), check:

 verified_count = sum (

 1 for c in self.all_contacts

 if (c.get( 'email_confidence' ) or '' ).upper() == 'VERIFIED'

 )

 if verified_count >= self.l15_step5_skip_threshold: # default 15

 logger.info(

 f"v2 L15 Step 5 skipped: {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts "

 f"already found (threshold={self.l15_step5_skip_threshold})"

 )

 return

 Threshold is a class attribute on Research JobV2 , default 15, overridable per-instance. Set conservatively (not aggressively).

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1 Claude batch call per triggered job = ~$0.025-0.05/job on easy targets only. Risk: very low — you already have 15+ VERIFIED contacts by the time this triggers; skipping

 Step 5 means not looking for MORE when you already have enough. Steps 1-4 still run, so existing INFERRED/LIKELY contacts still get upgraded to VERIFIED.

 Why no full L15 skip in Round 1: a more aggressive "skip L15 entirely when VERIFIED ≥ 20" optimization was considered but dropped. It would save more (~$0.10-0.20/job) but carries a subtle quality risk — L15

 Steps 1-4 upgrade existing INFERRED/LIKELY contacts to VERIFIED by search-and-verify. Skipping those upgrades means fewer verified emails on triggered runs. Deferred to Round 2 once A/B data shows how often

 the upgrades actually happen.

 (C) Entry point hook (minimal)

 Override Research JobV2.__init__ to accept an optional v2_config: dict parameter containing:

 - l15_step5_skip_threshold (default 15)

 - early_exit_verified_threshold (default 20)

 - enable_early_exit (default True)

 All parameters default to Round 1 values. Future rounds add more config keys without breaking the interface.

 ---

 Part 2 — A/B test harness

 File to create: scripts/ab_test_research_engines.py (new, ~150-200 lines)

 Purpose: run the same research target through v1 and v2 in sequence, compare results, output structured diff + save to log.

 Interface:

 .venv/bin/python scripts/ab_test_research_engines.py \

 --district "Cypress-Fairbanks ISD" --state TX

 What it does:

 1. Constructs both Research Job(district, state) (v1) and Research JobV2(district, state) (v2) with default configs

 2. Runs v1 first, captures full result dict + wall clock

 3. Runs v2 second, captures full result dict + wall clock

 4. Computes comparison metrics:

 - Contact overlap: contacts found by both engines (keyed on normalized name)

 - v1-unique: contacts only v1 found (potential v2 regressions)

 - v2-unique: contacts only v2 found (v2 wins)

 - VERIFIED email delta: count of VERIFIED emails in v1 vs v2, and overlap

 - Query count: Serper queries used per engine

 - Wall clock: seconds per engine

 - Estimated cost: derived from query count + estimated Claude token consumption per layer (from the layers_used list in each result)

 5. Prints a side-by-side comparison table

 6. Appends a structured JSON record to scripts/ab_results.jsonl for cumulative analysis

 Cost estimation: per-engine, derived:

 - Serper: queries_used × $0.001 (standard Serper plan)

 - Exa: exa_layer_hit_count × 2 queries × $0.005

 - Brave: brave_layer_hit_count × 2 queries × $0.005

 - Claude: num_pages_extracted × $0.045 (Sonnet 4.6 estimate from real token counts)

 - Label the estimate clearly as estimate — not billing truth

 What it does NOT do in Round 1:

 - No automated quality grading against Active Accounts ground truth (Round 2)

 - No batch mode (run one target at a time)

 - No regression gate — informational only

 Data to record in ab_results.jsonl (one line per A/B run):

 ---

 Part 3 — Telegram command wiring

 Files to modify: agent/main.py

 Changes:

 - Add a new /research_ab <district> command handler that dispatches to the A/B harness script via subprocess.run (or equivalent async pattern matching existing /research dispatch).

 - Leave the existing /research <district> command alone — it continues to run v1 via the singleton research_queue . No production behavior change in Round 1.

 - Do NOT wire research_queue.enqueue to the v2 engine yet. The 4 existing call sites ( main.py:542 , 1591 , 2480 , 2876 per exploration) stay on v1 until Round 2 after A/B data validates v2.

 Rationale: zero production risk. A/B testing is opt-in via the new command. If v2 has a bug, only the ab_test runs expose it — live prospecting stays unaffected.

 Insertion point: _on_user_message at agent/main.py:1564-1585 currently routes /research_district|research|look up|find contacts for|find contacts at via regex. Add a new regex branch for /research_ab that

 handles the A/B case separately.

 Risk: very low — additive command, doesn't change existing dispatch.

 ---

 Files summary

 Create (2):

 - tools/research_engine_v2.py — Research JobV2 subclass with Part 1 overrides

 - scripts/ab_test_research_engines.py — Part 2 A/B harness

 Modify (3):

 - tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 — _merge_contacts upgrade-on-collision (Part 0, shared)

 - tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235 — extract_from_multiple upgrade-on-collision (Part 0, shared)

 - agent/main.py — add /research_ab <district> command handler (Part 3)

 - ResearchQueue (still runs v1 by default — no config change)

 - All existing v1 layer implementations

 - BUG 4 diocesan playbook, BUG 5 contamination filter

 - research_engine.py::ResearchJob other methods (everything inherits cleanly)

 - All prod API keys, env vars, Railway deploy config

 ---

 Verification plan

 Test targets (already locked in Session 60):

 1. Cypress-Fairbanks ISD (TX, 118,470) — large

 2. Cincinnati Public Schools (OH, 34,860) — medium

 3. Conejo Valley USD (CA, 15,999) — medium

 4. Lake Zurich CUSD 95 (IL, 5,703) — small

 5. Waverly School District 145 (NE, 2,134) — small

 All 5 confirmed in territory_data master list, all cold (no Active Account presence verified via Part 0 Active Accounts lookup in scripts/s60_test_target_lookup.py ).

 Verification procedure after each Part ships:

 Part 0 verification (dedup fix):

 - Unit test: construct a synthetic list of contacts where new entry has email and existing didn't. Assert new email is preserved on merge. Assert confidence upgrade happens. Assert no duplication. Write as

 scripts/test_dedup_fix.py .

 - Integration test: run v1 on Cypress-Fairbanks ISD before and after the fix. Compare contact lists. Any VERIFIED email that appears after fix but not before is a recovered-silent-loss.

 Part 1 verification (v2 engine):

 - Sanity test: instantiate Research JobV2("Waverly School District 145", "NE") and run it. Assert it completes without exception. Assert it produces a non-empty result dict with the expected keys (contacts,

 total, with_email, queries_used, layers_used, elapsed_seconds).

 - Confirm override behavior: assert the URL dedup logger.info line fires when raw_pages contains duplicate URLs. Assert the L15 Step 5 skip triggers on a target that finds ≥15 VERIFIED contacts from Steps 1-4.

 - No separate quality test — quality is validated via the A/B harness in Part 2.

 Part 2 verification (A/B harness):

 - Run against Waverly (smallest, fastest). Assert the harness produces a valid JSONL line in scripts/ab_results.jsonl .

 - Inspect the output manually for sanity. Expected: v1 and v2 produce similar total counts, v2 uses fewer queries on easy targets.

 Part 3 verification (Telegram wiring):

 - Via Telethon bridge (see reference_telethon_bridge.md ): send /research_ab Waverly School District 145 from Claude Code to Scout, wait for reply, verify the reply contains the comparison output.

 Full Round 1 verification:

 - After all 4 parts ship, run /research_ab <district> against all 5 test targets in sequence.

 - Compile the 5 JSONL records into a summary table.

 - Expected outcomes (estimates):

 - v2 finds ≥95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts on every target (quality bar) — in practice should match or slightly exceed v1 because Part 0 dedup fix recovers silently-lost contacts for both engines equally

 - v2 uses ~5 fewer Serper queries on targets where L15 Step 5 is skipped (when VERIFIED ≥ 15)

 - v2 uses similar queries on targets where L15 Step 5 still runs

 - v2 cost estimate is 10-25% lower on average (from URL dedup saving L9 Claude calls on duplicate pages + L15 Step 5 skip on easy targets)

 - v2 wall clock is ~same or 5-10% lower (URL dedup avoids redundant Claude calls; no parallelism added in Round 1)

 Stop conditions — things that block Round 1 from shipping to production default:

 - Any target where v2 finds <95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts → regression, investigate before merging

 - Any target where v2 produces contacts that contain @ domains from OTHER districts (contamination filter regression) → BUG 5 broken, investigate

 - Wall clock regression >30% on any target → something serial is wrong

 - Any target where v2 raises an exception while v1 completes → immediate halt, investigate

 Success metric for Round 1:

 - All 5 targets produce clean A/B runs

 - v2 costs 15-35% less on average (estimate)

 - v2 quality matches or beats v1 on VERIFIED contact count

 - Zero regressions on BUG 4/5 filters

 - Round 1 unlocks Round 2 (cache, batching, potentially flipping /research default to v2)

 ---

 Round 1 per-job cost reduction: 10-25% (estimate — derived from the 3 surgical wins above, not yet measured. Cost savings come from Part 1 (A) URL dedup ~$0.05-0.15/job + (B) L15 Step 5 skip ~$0.025-0.05/job.

 Part 0 dedup fix is a quality gain not a cost change.)

 Round 1 quality delta: 0 to +5 VERIFIED emails per job (estimate — from Part 0 dedup fix recovering silently-lost contacts; actual value depends on how often the collision occurred in real runs)

 Round 1 wall clock: similar to v1, possibly 5-10% faster on L15-skip-triggered targets (estimate — no parallelism, same layer sequence otherwise)

 Round 1 budget fit: at ~100 atomic jobs/week saturation × ~$0.55/job (blended after Round 1 savings) = ~$55/week. Still above the $25/week hard cap. Round 1 alone does NOT hit the budget target — it's the

 foundation. The full $25/week target requires Round 2 (entity cache + Claude batching) + Round 3 (parallelism). Round 1's purpose is to ship safe wins, validate the A/B harness, and create the foundation for

 Round 2.

 Time to build: ~4-6 working hours total across all 4 parts.

 ---

 - Entity cache with 30-60 day TTL — keyed by (normalized_district, state), in-memory + /tmp + GitHub persistence. Potentially the biggest single cost saver on repeat runs. Deferred because: (a) need to avoid

 polluting the A/B test with cache hits in Round 1, (b) non-trivial module to write correctly with TTL + invalidation semantics.

 - L9 Claude call batching — group 5-10 pages per Claude call instead of 1 per page. Batch mode only (adds latency, interactive stays 1-per-call). Deferred because: (a) needs careful prompt template rewrite +

 validation, (b) Round 1 A/B data tells us whether the savings are worth the complexity.

 - Haiku 4.5 fallback for L9 extraction on clearly-structured pages. Deferred because: (a) needs a classifier for "structured" vs "ambiguous," (b) quality risk until measured against v1 baseline in Round 1.

 - L16/L17 conditional execution — only run Exa if L9 first pass produces <15 VERIFIED contacts. Deferred because: (a) requires Phase A reordering in the engine (L16 currently runs in Phase A parallel, would

 need to move to Phase D conditional), (b) Round 1's simpler L15 skip tests the concept first.

 - Job-level parallelism — 4-way concurrent ResearchQueue worker pool with shared Serper/Exa/Brave token bucket. Pure speed win, 0 cost change. Deferred because: (a) needs rate-limit budget design, (b) campaign

 UX ( /sweep all_districts IL ) is a separate feature.

 ---

 - Overnight batch scheduler — auto-route campaigns with >10 entities to run 6pm-6am CST, report results in morning brief

 - Territory master list L0 — persist discovered district domains to territory_data so L4 becomes a cache lookup on repeat runs

 - Adaptive escalation — proper phase-by-phase yield checkpoints instead of the single Round 1 L15 skip

 - Tier system by enrollment — only viable once the Research Log state field is fixed upstream to store 2-letter abbreviations so lookup_district_enrollment doesn't fail 50% of the time

 ---

 Out of scope / explicit non-goals

 - Do NOT flip /research default to v2 in Round 1. That happens in Round 2 after A/B data confirms quality parity.

 - Do NOT retire v1 in any round. v1 stays in the codebase forever as the A/B control and as a fallback for any target v2 regresses on.

 - Do NOT modify v1's layer implementations except for the shared Part 0 dedup fix.

 - Do NOT remove Firecrawl L18/L19 — they already graceful-skip when FIRECRAWL_API_KEY isn't set (confirmed in Railway: not set). They're dead code in production today and harmless.

 - Do NOT touch the diocesan playbook, contamination filter, or BUG 4/5 logic. These shipped in Sessions 55-57 and should not be disturbed without a specific reason.

 - Do NOT add new external dependencies (no new Python packages).

 - Do NOT write a Google Doc design spec — Steven said "we can do a really long in-depth fix another time." Round 1 is surgical, not architectural.

 ---

 Open questions / Steven sign-off needed

 None. This plan is based on real measurement from the Research Log, confirmed insertion points from the exploration agents, and the constraints Steven established in the Session 60 conversation. Ready to

 execute on approval.

 ---

 Post-approval execution order

 1. Commit 1: Part 0 shared dedup fix ( _merge_contacts + extract_from_multiple ), unit tests in scripts/test_dedup_fix.py , verification via integration test against Waverly.

 2. Commit 2: Part 1 tools/research_engine_v2.py with Research JobV2 subclass, overriding run() , _layer9_claude_extraction , _layer15_email_verification , and __init__ .

 3. Commit 3: Part 2 scripts/ab_test_research_engines.py A/B harness.

 4. Commit 4: Part 3 agent/main.py /research_ab command handler.

 5. Verification run: A/B test all 5 target districts, compile results, report to Steven.

 6. Decision point: if Round 1 verification passes the success metric, Steven approves Round 2 plan. If it fails or shows unexpected behavior, iterate on Round 1 before Round 2.

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 Refine this plan. Your job is to pressure-test it honestly — keep what's
 genuinely strong, rebuild what isn't, and hand back something I can execute
 with full confidence.
 Act as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Be direct,

 specific, and opinionated. No hedging, no diplomacy, no soft language around

 real problems. If something is wrong, say it's wrong and say why. But the

 inverse also holds: if something is right, leave it alone. Do not rewrite solid

 work just to look thorough — churn for its own sake is its own kind of

 failure. The bar for changing something is that the new version is meaningfully

 better, not merely different.

 happen because the reviewer started reacting before they finished

 understanding.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Is the overall approach actually right, or is it

 just the first thing that came to mind? Interrogate the architecture, the

 framing, and the core assumptions before touching details. If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — different architecture, different sequencing,

 different tradeoffs, a smaller problem hiding inside the big one — say so and

 restructure around it. Do not anchor to your first answer because you already

 committed to it on the page.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it tomorrow. Walk the plan

 end to end and ask: could I actually sit down and build this without hitting a

 wall? Find the hidden assumptions, the vague steps, the dependency tangles, the

 places where things will quietly break at the edges, the spots where "it'll

 just work" is doing real load-bearing work. For every problem: name it, explain

 why it happens, explain what it costs, and give the fix. Diagnosis without

 prescription is useless.

 3. Pressure-test every technical decision against the best available knowledge.

 Architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — for each one, ask

 whether it's genuinely the strongest option or just the most familiar. Weigh

 it against what actually holds up in production, not what's popular in

 tutorials or trending this quarter. Where real tradeoffs exist between

 competing approaches, surface them honestly and take a clear position on which

 one I should pick and why. No "it depends" cop-outs.

 4. Optimize for the highest-quality result, reached as fast and efficiently as

 possible. Every step should be pulling its weight toward a correct, durable,

 high-quality outcome — not just toward motion. Ruthlessly cut anything that

 adds time without adding quality, and ruthlessly add anything that protects

 quality even if it costs a little time upfront. Sequence the work so that the

 decisions and pieces with the highest leverage on the final result come first,

 so that mistakes get caught as early and cheaply as possible, and so that

 nothing downstream has to be redone because something upstream was rushed.

 Where two paths exist, pick the one that gets to the correct answer with the

 least wasted motion — and say why. Speed comes from not doing the wrong work,

 not from skipping the right work.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth is welcome. Redundancy, vagueness, and

 ceremony are not. Every element earns its place or it goes. If a step would

 make me stop and ask "what does this actually mean in practice," either make it

 concrete or delete it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them. If a

 section exists because it sounds thorough rather than because it changes the

 outcome, kill it.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that looks minor now and will hurt later? Edge cases, error handling,

 failure modes, prerequisites, migration paths, ordering constraints, and the

 easy-to-punt decisions that get expensive to reverse. Surface them now, while

 they're cheap.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch. Do not patch the original — rebuild it

 with everything above baked in. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Preserve the parts of the original that were already strong —

 rebuilding does not mean discarding what worked. The goal is the best possible

 plan, not the most rewritten one. Then, at the end, list the most important

 changes you made and explain the reasoning behind each one so I can judge

 whether your thinking holds up.

 Take your time. Think deeply. This is the version I'm going to execute from —

 make it the best one you're capable of producing.
