 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Done reading. Here are the specific wins I found, each with file:line and honest estimates.

 ---

 5 concrete wins I can point at in the code

 Win #1 — L18 and L19 are BOTH running Firecrawl on the same district site (waste)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1095-1266

 L18 ( _layer18_firecrawl_extract ) calls fc.extract(urls=[f"https://{domain}/*"], enable_web_search=True) which crawls the district site AND does a web search, then extracts contacts directly (bypassing Claude).

 L19 ( _layer19_firecrawl_site_map ) separately calls fc.map() + up to 8 fc.scrape() calls on the same district site, then the scraped markdown flows into L9 Claude extraction.

 Both target the same domain with overlapping purpose. L18 and L19 are redundant — either one of them (if it works) covers the staff directory case. Running both means paying Firecrawl twice for the same

 district.

 Plus: L18 has enable_web_search=True set (line 1150), which doubles its cost. Web search is already done by L1/L2/L3/L5/L11/L14/L16/L20 — adding another web search pass inside Firecrawl is redundant.

 Fix: pick ONE. I'd keep L19 (map + scrape → raw markdown → L9 Claude extraction with full context visibility) and disable L18 entirely, OR if L18 stays, set enable_web_search=False .

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.20/job (estimate — Firecrawl's /extract with enable_web_search=True is one of their more expensive operations; exact savings depend on their current pricing which I'd verify before

 shipping). Risk: zero — if L19 alone is insufficient we can flip back.

 ---

 Win #2 — raw_pages is never URL-deduped before L9 Claude extraction

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:_add_raw_from_serper at line 1566, and the raw_pages.append(...) calls scattered through L1-L20.

 Multiple layers can return the SAME URL for the same district:

 - L1 direct-title search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page

 - L11 school-staff search: Austin ISD → their staff directory page (same URL)

 - L16 Exa broad: Austin ISD → possibly same page

 - L4 district-site search: also sometimes

 Each gets appended to self.raw_pages independently. The 2-pass content filter at L9 (lines 680-702) does NOT dedupe by URL, only by content signals. So the SAME page can get sent to Claude 2-3 times in one

 job, costing 2-3x the extraction money, with identical output being deduped at _merge_contacts afterwards.

 Fix: dedupe self.raw_pages by URL before L9 runs. ~5 lines of code at the top of _layer9_claude_extraction :

 seen_urls = set ()

 deduped = []

 for url, content in self.raw_pages:

 if url not in seen_urls:

 seen_urls.add(url)

 deduped.append((url, content))

 self.raw_pages = deduped

 Estimated savings: ~$0.05-0.15/job (estimate — depends on actual URL overlap across layers, which I haven't measured. Could verify with a one-line logger.info before shipping). Risk: zero — removes only

 duplicate work, no quality change.

 ---

 Win #3 — L9 calls Claude Sonnet one page at a time, no batching

 Where: tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 def extract_from_multiple(pages: list [ tuple [ str , str ]], district_name: str ) -> list [ dict ]:

 all_contacts = []

 seen = set ()

 for url, content in pages:

 contacts = extract_contacts(content, url, district_name) # ONE Sonnet call per page

 ...

 Each call to extract_contacts fires a separate Claude Sonnet 4.6 API request with a ~400-token system prompt ( EXTRACT_SYSTEM at lines 55-104). For a typical job with 20 pages surviving the 2-pass filter,

 that's 20 separate Sonnet calls with identical system prompts.

 Per-call overhead: 400 system tokens × 20 calls = 8,000 tokens of redundant prompt input (~$0.024 wasted on input alone).

 Fix: rewrite extract_from_multiple to batch. Send 5-10 pages per Claude call in a single prompt like "Extract contacts from these 5 pages: [page 1 URL]... [page 1 content]... [page 2 URL]... [page 2

 content]..." with structured output expecting one contact array. Sonnet handles multi-doc extraction cleanly at 200K context. For 20 pages → 4 batched calls instead of 20.

 Estimated savings: ~$0.10-0.30/job (estimate — reduction comes from (a) ~75% fewer system-prompt tokens, (b) Sonnet's per-request overhead amortized, (c) faster wall clock. Not a 75% cost cut because per-page

 content tokens are still the same). Risk: low — Sonnet handles multi-doc inputs well, but the prompt template and JSON output shape need careful rewriting to keep extraction accuracy at parity. A/B test

 against the existing output on the 5 test targets would validate.

 Caveat Steven already flagged: batching adds latency to interactive /research X (wait for all 5 pages before calling Claude) — so this should be batch_mode=True only for the overnight path, and the interactive

 path keeps single-page calls. Gated.

 ---

 Win #4 — L15 Step 5 "discovery" runs unconditionally even when we already have plenty

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1487-1519

 L15's Step 5 does 5 @domain Serper queries to find MORE contacts we might have missed (L1-L14 output), then feeds the results through another extract_from_multiple Claude call. Runs every time, regardless of

 how many VERIFIED contacts L9 already found.

 For "easy" districts where L9 already returned 20+ VERIFIED contacts, Step 5 is searching for more contacts we don't need — marginal yield is typically 0-2 extras.

 Fix: conditional skip. If all_contacts already has ≥10 contacts with email_confidence=VERIFIED (or some threshold you're comfortable with), skip Step 5. Keep Steps 1-4 (those improve existing contacts, not

 just pad the list).

 # ── Step 5: Discovery searches using @domain pattern ─────────────────

 if domain and _l15_used < _L15_MAX and self._serper_count < self._serper_cap:

 verified_count = sum ( 1 for c in self.all_contacts if c.get( 'email_confidence' ) == 'VERIFIED' )

 if verified_count >= 10 :

 logger.info( f"L15 Step 5 skipped: already have {verified_count} VERIFIED contacts" )

 else :

 remaining = min (_L15_MAX - _l15_used, 5 )

 ...

 Estimated savings: ~5 Serper queries + 1-2 Claude batches per "easy" job = ~$0.02-0.05/job on easy targets only (estimate — only applies when the threshold triggers). Risk: very low — you explicitly set the

 "we have enough" threshold.

 ---

 Win #5 — _merge_contacts silently drops higher-quality duplicates (THIS IS A QUALITY BUG, not a cost bug)

 Where: tools/research_engine.py:1635-1649 AND tools/contact_extractor.py:214-235

 Both _merge_contacts (in research_engine.py ) and extract_from_multiple (in contact_extractor.py ) dedupe contacts by (first_name, last_name) . If page 1 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, (no email)" and page

 2 returns "John Smith, Superintendent, john.smith@district.edu, VERIFIED" — the page 2 record is DROPPED because John Smith is already in the seen set from page 1.

 This is a silent quality loss. Real scenario: L1 staff directory page has names but no emails. L11 school-staff page has the same name WITH email. Current code keeps the no-email version from L1.

 Fix: upgrade-on-merge logic. If a new contact has the same name as an existing one but has BETTER data (email when existing had none, VERIFIED when existing was INFERRED, etc.), REPLACE the existing record.

 def _merge_contacts(self, new_contacts: list [ dict ]):

 existing_by_key = {}

 for c in self.all_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 conf_rank = { "VERIFIED" : 4 , "LIKELY" : 3 , "INFERRED" : 2 , "UNKNOWN" : 1 , "" : 0 }

 for c in new_contacts:

 key = f"{c.get('first_name','').lower()}|{c.get('last_name','').lower()}"

 if key not in existing_by_key:

 existing_by_key[key] = c

 self.all_contacts.append(c)

 else :

 old = existing_by_key[key]

 old_conf = conf_rank.get(old.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 new_conf = conf_rank.get(c.get( 'email_confidence' , '' ).upper(), 0 )

 # Upgrade if new has email and old didn't, or new has better confidence

 if (c.get( 'email' ) and not old.get( 'email' )) or new_conf > old_conf:

 old.update({k: v for k, v in c.items() if v})

 Estimated impact: ZERO cost savings, but non-trivial quality gain — potentially recovers 1-5 VERIFIED emails per job that the current code silently loses. Worth doing because "as good as possible" is your bar.

 Risk: very low — upgrading is strictly better than dropping.

 ---

 My recommended order to ship

 1. Win #2 (URL dedup) — tiny diff, zero risk, measurable savings immediately. ~30 min work.

 2. Win #5 (merge-upgrade) — tiny diff, pure quality gain. ~45 min work.

 3. Win #1 (L18 disable or enable_web_search=False ) — one-line change, measurable savings. ~15 min work.

 4. Win #4 (L15 Step 5 skip-when-enough) — ~10 line conditional. ~30 min work.

 5. Win #3 (L9 batching) — biggest cost win but requires careful rewrite + validation. ~half day work, gated to batch mode only.

 Wins 1-4 together: ~2 hours of work, estimated ~$0.15-0.40/job savings, zero quality risk, plus recovers some lost VERIFIED emails.

 Win 3 alone: ~half day work, estimated additional ~$0.10-0.30/job savings, low risk with A/B validation, batch-mode only.

 ---

 My proposal: start with Wins #1, #2, #4, and #5 right now as separate small commits. They're surgical, low-risk, and we can measure the combined effect by running one of the 5 test targets through both engines

 (old commit vs new commit on the same target).

 Then we evaluate, and if the numbers look good, we take on Win #3 as a slightly bigger piece of work.

 Do you want me to start building Wins #1/#2/#4/#5 as 4 small commits right now? Or adjust the order / add / remove anything first?

 not a current cost.

 ⎿   Total Research Log rows: 29

 Sample notes field (col H) for 8 most recent public district rows:

 Desert Sands Unified: 7m 4s | 33 queries | L2:title-variations:13 | L1:direct-title:4 | L14:conference:4 | L15:email-verify:1 | L3:linkedin:12 | L5:news-grants:3 | L11:school-staff:1

 … +8 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Huge finding from Research Log notes field. The per-layer contact yield data is stored there and I just got a first look at real production numbers:

 ┌──────────────────────┬──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ District │ Size │ Top yielding layers (contacts/layer) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Houston ISD │ 200k │ L16 Exa-broad: 39 / L17 Exa-domain: 17 / L15: 10 / L1-L3: 12 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Columbus City │ 45k │ L16 Exa-broad: 58 / L17 Exa-domain: 15 / L13 state-doe: 5 / L1-L3: 6 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Leander ISD │ 40k │ L15: 13 / L1 direct: 5 / L2 variations: 5 / L16: 3 / L3-L5-L14: 4 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Guthrie Public │ ~2k │ L16: 25 / L15: 10 / L13: 9 / L2: 3 / L17: 5 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Desert Sands │ 27k │ L2 variations: 13 / L3 LinkedIn: 12 / L1: 4 / L14: 4 / L5: 3 / L15: 1 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Irving STEAM │ ~2k │ L2: 10 / L14: 3 / L4: 1 (everything else: 0) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Lackland ISD (retry) │ 7k │ L16: 18 / L2: 5 / L14: 2 / L15: 2 │

 └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 I have enough to write the plan. Entering plan mode.

 ⏺ Entered plan mode

 Claude is now exploring and designing an implementation approach.

 ⏺ Dispatching Phase 1 exploration agents in parallel. Two agents, one for real production per-layer data, one for insertion points + existing utilities.

 ⏺ 2 Explore agents finished (ctrl+o to expand)

 ├─ Aggregate Research Log per-layer yield · 19 tool uses · 55.8k tokens

 │ ⎿ Done

 └─ Map v2 engine insertion points + existing utilities · 40 tool uses · 66.3k tokens

 ⎿ Done

 ⏺ Agent 2 gave me everything I needed — clean answers on insertion points, cache patterns, dedup bug confirmation, and A/B harness absence. Agent 1 unfortunately drafted a script instead of running one. Let me

 pull the aggregated per-layer data myself via inline Python (plan mode allows read-only bash).

 ⏺ Bash (.venv/bin/python -c "

 import os, json, re, sys…)

 ⎿   Public district rows: 20

 === PER-LAYER AGGREGATES (public districts) ===

 … +38 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit

 rate but dominates when it hits (mean 28.6 contacts). L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low hit rate (10-35%) and low mean yield — dead weight candidates. And territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment is failing on 10

 of 20 jobs because the state field in the Research Log is truncated ("Te", "Oh", "Ok" instead of "TX", "OH", "OK") — that's a data quality issue in the log, not an engine problem, but it means a size-based

 tier system would misfire.

 ⏺ Updated plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ Plan written to ~/.claude/plans/spicy-sleeping-gadget.md . Let me do a quick self-review before calling Exit Plan Mode — checking for placeholders, contradictions, scope creep, and ambiguity.

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Self-review caught one real issue: Part 1 (C) "early exit" overlaps with (B) and has subtle quality risk — skipping L15 entirely means skipping Steps 1-4 which can upgrade INFERRED contacts to VERIFIED. That's

 a small quality sacrifice I shouldn't take in Round 1. Tightening the plan.

 1 alone doesn't hit $25/week target, Round 2 explicitly needed. Calling Exit Plan Mode to present for approval.

 Thinking out loud before I touch the plan file — what's actually wrong with v1 of this plan:

 11 tasks ( 4 done, 1 in progress, 6 open)
 ◼ Ask Steven clarifying questions one at a time
 P opose 2-3 approaches with t adeoffs
 Pr sent design in sections for Steven's approval
 Write design doc to docs/superpowers/sp cs/
 Spec self-review (placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope)
 ◻ User reviews spec — Steven sign-off gate
 ◻ Transition to writing-plans skill
 Explore research engine context
 ✔ Bank public-districts + no-diocese-default memory files
 ✔ Read full Scout strategy stack context (F1-F9, stages, roadmap)
 … +1 completed

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Thinking out loud before I touch the plan file — what's actually wrong with v1 of this plan:

 The architecture is wrong. I chose "subclass Research Job as Research JobV2 " reflexively because it sounded clean. It isn't. Overriding run() means copying the 50-line phase orchestration because I can't modify
 it mid-flow from a subclass. Overriding _layer15_email_verification means copying 200+ lines to change one if statement near the end. That's not isolation — it's code duplication masquerading as encapsulation.
 The right shape s featur flag i v1 itself, defaulting to old behavior. A/B test becomes: instantiate two ResearchJob objects, one with flags on, one with flags off. No v2 file. Steven's "preserve the old
 way" c nstraint is sati fied bec use defaults-off flags preserve byte-for-byte current behavior. I should not have defaulted to subclass-for-isolation without thinking about what isolation actually costs.

 11 tasks ( 4 done, 1 in progress, 6 open)
 ◼ Ask Steven clarifying questions one at a time
 Propos 2-3 ap roaches with tradeo fs
 P esen design in sections for Steven's approval
 ◻ Write design doc to docs/superpowers/specs/
 ◻ Spec self-review (placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope)
 ◻ User reviews spec — Steven sign-off gate
 ◻ Transition to writing-plans skill
 ✔ Explore research engine context
 ✔ Bank public-districts + no-diocese-default memory files
 ✔ Read full Scout strategy stack context (F1-F9, stages, roadmap)
 … +1 completed

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Part 3 (Telegram command) is scope creep. The A/B harness is a local development tool — I run it from Claude Code via .venv/bin/python . Steven doesn't need a /research_ab Telegram command because he's not the
 one running A/B tests. Round 1 shouldn't touch agent/main.py at all. That removes an entire commit and reduces production-path risk.

 11 tasks ( 4 done, 1 in progress, 6 open)
 ◼ Ask Steven clarifying questions one at a time
 Propo 2 3 approaches with trade ffs
 Pr sent d sign in sec ions for Steven's approval
 W ite design doc o docs/superpowers/specs/
 ◻ Spec self-review (placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope)
 ◻ User reviews spec — Steven sign-off gate
 ◻ Transition to writing-plans skill
 ✔ Explore research engine context
 ✔ Bank public-districts + no-diocese-default memory files
 ✔ Read full Scout strategy stack context (F1-F9, stages, roadmap)
 … +1 completed

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 The cost estimation is a guess masquerading as a measurement. I wrote "~$0.045/page" from memory. Real Claude API calls return response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens — the SDK gives us
 exact token counts on every call. The research engine doesn't log them today, which means my A/B harness would "estimate" costs from a formula rather than reading actual billing-grade numbers. The foundation
 Round 1 needs is token logging, not cost formulas. Instrument contact_extractor.extract_contacts to capture response.usage on every call, log it with the job, and the A/B harness reads the real numbers.

 … +1 completed

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 The verification plan is vague. "Sanity test — instantiate and run" isn't a test, it's a smoke check. Each specific behavior change needs a unit test with the failure case explicitly constructed: Part 0's
 dedup fix needs a test where page 1 returns "John Smith no email" and page 2 returns "John Smith + VERIFIED email" and the assertion is that the final contact has the email. Part 1(A)'s URL dedup needs three
 cases (exact du lic te, trailing slash, query string difference) because real URLs have those variations. Part 1(B)'s L15 skip needs two cases (14 VERIFIED → runs, 15 VERIFIED → skipped). Without these, the
 integration test agains 5 targets is checking the wrong thing — it's measuring yield, not correctness.

 Success metrics are floors, not gates. "≥95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts" is directionally right but not numerically specific enough to serve as a ship/no-ship gate. A senior review asks "what EXACTLY fails
 Round 1 acceptance?" and the plan should answer that with three per-target predicates joined by AND: verified_contacts ≥ v1 × 0.95 AND cost ≤ v1 × 0.90 AND wall_clock ≤ v1 × 1.05 . All three, every target. One
 miss blocks Round 2.

 Part 0 is safer than I said but the rollout needs a discipline. Modifying shared code that both the default engine AND the new flagged paths depend on is a production-path change. "Very low risk" is too glib.
 The discipline: Part 0 ships FIRST, standalone, with unit tests, and gets integration-tested on one of the 5 targets (v1-pre vs v1-post) BEFORE any other changes touch the file. If the fix produces fewer
 verified contacts than the bug, th ix is wrong. Only after Part 0 is green does Part 1 start.

 Round 1 doesn't hit the budget and I should say why that's fine. $0.15-0.20/job savings against a $0.55/job blended baseline is ~25% cost reduction. At ~100 jobs/week saturation that's $55/week → $41/week.
 Still 64% over the $25/week cap. Round 1's job is NOT to solve the budget problem — it's to (a) fix the two silent quality bugs, (b) ship the cheap low-risk wins, (c) build the A/B harness that validates v2
 int insic behavio , and (d) create the foundation for Round 2 where the real cost levers live (entity cache, Claude batching, model swap on high-confidence pages, job-level parallelism). Round 1 is a
 pre quisit , not the endgame. The pla needs to state this explicitly with honest numbers, not hand-wave with "foundation."

 import os, json, sys…)

 ⎿   Total Research Log rows: 29

 Sample notes field (col H) for 8 most recent public district rows:

 Desert Sands Unified: 7m 4s | 33 queries | L2:title-variations:13 | L1:direct-title:4 | L14:conference:4 | L15:email-verify:1 | L3:linkedin:12 | L5:news-grants:3 | L11:school-staff:1

 … +8 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Huge finding from Research Log notes field. The per-layer contact yield data is stored there and I just got a first look at real production numbers:

 ┌──────────────────────┬──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ District │ Size │ Top yielding layers (contacts/layer) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Houston ISD │ 200k │ L16 Exa-broad: 39 / L17 Exa-domain: 17 / L15: 10 / L1-L3: 12 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Columbus City │ 45k │ L16 Exa-broad: 58 / L17 Exa-domain: 15 / L13 state-doe: 5 / L1-L3: 6 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Leander ISD │ 40k │ L15: 13 / L1 direct: 5 / L2 variations: 5 / L16: 3 / L3-L5-L14: 4 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Guthrie Public │ ~2k │ L16: 25 / L15: 10 / L13: 9 / L2: 3 / L17: 5 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Desert Sands │ 27k │ L2 variations: 13 / L3 LinkedIn: 12 / L1: 4 / L14: 4 / L5: 3 / L15: 1 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Irving STEAM │ ~2k │ L2: 10 / L14: 3 / L4: 1 (everything else: 0) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Lackland ISD (retry) │ 7k │ L16: 18 / L2: 5 / L14: 2 / L15: 2 │

 └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 I have enough to write the plan. Entering plan mode.

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit

 ⏺ Self-review pass complete. Plan tightened — Part 1 (C) early-exit removed (quality risk), cost reduction estimate corrected from 15-35% to 10-25% (matches the actual 3 wins), budget math updated to show Round

 1 alone doesn't hit $25/week target, Round 2 explicitly needed. Calling Exit Plan Mode to present for approval.

 ⎿  Tool use rejected

 ⏺ Understood. Going to hold the full plan in head first, then rewrite from scratch.

 ---

 Thinking out loud before I touch the plan file — what's actually wrong with v1 of this plan:

 The architecture is wrong. I chose "subclass Research Job as Research JobV2 " reflexively because it sounded clean. It isn't. Overriding run() means copying the 50-line phase orchestration because I can't modify

 it mid-flow from a subclass. Overriding _layer15_email_verification means copying 200+ lines to change one if statement near the end. That's not isolation — it's code duplication masquerading as encapsulation.

 The right shape is feature flags in v1 itself, defaulting to old behavior. A/B test becomes: instantiate two ResearchJob objects, one with flags on, one with flags off. No v2 file. Steven's "preserve the old

 way" constraint is satisfied because defaults-off flags preserve byte-for-byte current behavior. I should not have defaulted to subclass-for-isolation without thinking about what isolation actually costs.

 Part 3 (Telegram command) is scope creep. The A/B harness is a local development tool — I run it from Claude Code via .venv/bin/python . Steven doesn't need a /research_ab Telegram command because he's not the

 one running A/B tests. Round 1 shouldn't touch agent/main.py at all. That removes an entire commit and reduces production-path risk.

 The cost estimation is a guess masquerading as a measurement. I wrote "~$0.045/page" from memory. Real Claude API calls return response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens — the SDK gives us

 exact token counts on every call. The research engine doesn't log them today, which means my A/B harness would "estimate" costs from a formula rather than reading actual billing-grade numbers. The foundation

 Round 1 needs is token logging, not cost formulas. Instrument contact_extractor.extract_contacts to capture response.usage on every call, log it with the job, and the A/B harness reads the real numbers.

 The verification plan is vague. "Sanity test — instantiate and run" isn't a test, it's a smoke check. Each specific behavior change needs a unit test with the failure case explicitly constructed: Part 0's

 dedup fix needs a test where page 1 returns "John Smith no email" and page 2 returns "John Smith + VERIFIED email" and the assertion is that the final contact has the email. Part 1(A)'s URL dedup needs three

 cases (exact duplicate, trailing slash, query string difference) because real URLs have those variations. Part 1(B)'s L15 skip needs two cases (14 VERIFIED → runs, 15 VERIFIED → skipped). Without these, the

 integration test against 5 targets is checking the wrong thing — it's measuring yield, not correctness.

 Success metrics are floors, not gates. "≥95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts" is directionally right but not numerically specific enough to serve as a ship/no-ship gate. A senior review asks "what EXACTLY fails

 Round 1 acceptance?" and the plan should answer that with three per-target predicates joined by AND: verified_contacts ≥ v1 × 0.95 AND cost ≤ v1 × 0.90 AND wall_clock ≤ v1 × 1.05 . All three, every target. One

 miss blocks Round 2.

 Part 0 is safer than I said but the rollout needs a discipline. Modifying shared code that both the default engine AND the new flagged paths depend on is a production-path change. "Very low risk" is too glib.

 The discipline: Part 0 ships FIRST, standalone, with unit tests, and gets integration-tested on one of the 5 targets (v1-pre vs v1-post) BEFORE any other changes touch the file. If the fix produces fewer

 verified contacts than the bug, the fix is wrong. Only after Part 0 is green does Part 1 start.

 Round 1 doesn't hit the budget and I should say why that's fine. $0.15-0.20/job savings against a $0.55/job blended baseline is ~25% cost reduction. At ~100 jobs/week saturation that's $55/week → $41/week.

 Still 64% over the $25/week cap. Round 1's job is NOT to solve the budget problem — it's to (a) fix the two silent quality bugs, (b) ship the cheap low-risk wins, (c) build the A/B harness that validates v2

 intrinsic behavior, and (d) create the foundation for Round 2 where the real cost levers live (entity cache, Claude batching, model swap on high-confidence pages, job-level parallelism). Round 1 is a

 prerequisite, not the endgame. The plan needs to state this explicitly with honest numbers, not hand-wave with "foundation."

 The missing things a senior reviewer would catch:

 1. No .gitignore entry for the A/B results file — it'll get committed accidentally

 2. No rate-limit handling in the A/B harness — two serial runs of ~57 Serper queries back-to-back can clip the 60/min rate limit; need a ≥30s delay between v1 and v2

 3. No handling of the case where v1 or v2 raises mid-run — harness should capture the exception and record it, not crash

 4. No way to run the A/B offline against cached results — every A/B run costs real API money even when debugging the harness itself. Need a fixture mode.

 5. No stopping condition — if v2 finds 0 contacts on a target while v1 finds 40, the A/B run should halt immediately and surface the target

 6. No cost ceiling — what if the harness itself costs $10 to run? Need a cap.

 7. The L15 Step 5 threshold ( 15 VERIFIED ) is a guess, not tuned. Real data showed median with_email is 5 and mean is 22 — a 15-threshold will rarely trigger. A data-driven threshold would be the median plus a

 margin, which for this distribution might be closer to 10.

 8. response.usage only works if we capture it per-call. If extract_from_multiple batches calls (it doesn't today), the attribution gets fuzzy.

 What's actually strong and should be preserved:

 - The 5 test districts (Cypress-Fairbanks, Cincinnati, Conejo Valley, Lake Zurich, Waverly) are the right set. Keep.

 - The measurement data (per-layer aggregates, L16/L17 variance, dedup bugs) is solid and anchors everything. Keep.

 - The Part 0 shared dedup fix is the right first move. Keep (with better test discipline).

 - The "no subclass, just preserve behavior" instinct is right — I just picked the wrong mechanism.

 - The Round 2/3 deferrals are correct — cache + batching + parallelism don't belong in Round 1.

 - The 5-target A/B verification against v1 is the right validation step.

 What's actually the right Round 1:

 1. Part 0: Dedup fix in contact_extractor.extract_from_multiple and ResearchJob._merge_contacts , with unit tests and pre-merge integration check against one target.

 2. Part 1: Three feature flags in ResearchJob.__init__ , all defaulting to preserved-v1-behavior:

 - enable_url_dedup: bool = False

 - l15_step5_skip_threshold: int | None = None

 - log_claude_usage: bool = False

 3. Part 2: A/B harness script that instantiates two ResearchJob objects (flags-off vs flags-on), runs serially with 30s delay, reads response.usage via the new log_claude_usage flag, computes real costs,

 writes to /tmp/scout_ab_results.jsonl , handles exceptions, enforces cost ceiling.

 Three parts, three commits, no agent/main.py changes, no v2 file, real measurement instead of estimates, numerical success gates, defensive rollout.

 ⏺ Wait — re-reading the usage logging design I realize thread-local storage has a concurrency bug. extract_from_multiple is called via run_in_executor (confirmed at tools/research_engine.py:707 ), which means it
 runs in the thread pool executor, NOT the main asyncio thread. Setting threading.local() in ResearchJob.run() (main thread) creates a TLS entry for the main thread that the executor thread never sees. Logging
 would silently no-o . Fixing.

 ⏺ Huge finding from Research Log notes field. The per-layer contact yield data is stored there and I just got a first look at real production numbers:

 ┌──────────────────────┬──────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ District │ Size │ Top yielding layers (contacts/layer) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Houston ISD │ 200k │ L16 Exa-broad: 39 / L17 Exa-domain: 17 / L15: 10 / L1-L3: 12 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Columbus City │ 45k │ L16 Exa-broad: 58 / L17 Exa-domain: 15 / L13 state-doe: 5 / L1-L3: 6 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Leander ISD │ 40k │ L15: 13 / L1 direct: 5 / L2 variations: 5 / L16: 3 / L3-L5-L14: 4 combined │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Guthrie Public │ ~2k │ L16: 25 / L15: 10 / L13: 9 / L2: 3 / L17: 5 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Desert Sands │ 27k │ L2 variations: 13 / L3 LinkedIn: 12 / L1: 4 / L14: 4 / L5: 3 / L15: 1 │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Irving STEAM │ ~2k │ L2: 10 / L14: 3 / L4: 1 (everything else: 0) │

 ├──────────────────────┼──────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Lackland ISD (retry) │ 7k │ L16: 18 / L2: 5 / L14: 2 / L15: 2 │

 └──────────────────────┴──────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Load-bearing insight: L16 Exa-broad is the single biggest contact producer for large districts (39-58 contacts each on Houston and Columbus). It's not a minor layer — it's often the PRIMARY source of yield.

 BUT — it's also near-worthless for some targets (Desert Sands 27k got 0 from L16, Leander 40k got only 3).

 And Exa isn't just a direct cost — each Exa result is a full 15k-char page that gets fed into L9 Claude extraction. So L16/L17 indirectly amplify L9 Claude cost by 10-20 extra page extractions per job. When

 L16 is on, L9 fires 10-20 additional Sonnet calls. That's where most of the current cost actually lives.

 This completely reframes the optimization. The biggest lever isn't "make L9 cheaper" — it's "don't trigger L16/L17 when they won't yield." And we can decide that up-front based on enrollment: small districts

 don't benefit from semantic search, large ones do.

 margin, which for this distribution might be closer to 10.

 8. response.usage only works if we capture it per-call. If extract_from_multiple batches calls (it doesn't today), the attribution gets fuzzy.

 What's actually strong and should be preserved:

 - The 5 test districts (Cypress-Fairbanks, Cincinnati, Conejo Valley, Lake Zurich, Waverly) are the right set. Keep.

 - The measurement data (per-layer aggregates, L16/L17 variance, dedup bugs) is solid and anchors everything. Keep.

 - The Part 0 shared dedup fix is the right first move. Keep (with better test discipline).

 - The "no subclass, just preserve behavior" instinct is right — I just picked the wrong mechanism.

 - The Round 2/3 deferrals are correct — cache + batching + parallelism don't belong in Round 1.

 - The 5-target A/B verification against v1 is the right validation step.

 What's actually the right Round 1:

 1. Part 0: Dedup fix in contact_extractor.extract_from_multiple and ResearchJob._merge_contacts , with unit tests and pre-merge integration check against one target.

 2. Part 1: Three feature flags in ResearchJob.__init__ , all defaulting to preserved-v1-behavior:

 - enable_url_dedup: bool = False

 - l15_step5_skip_threshold: int | None = None

 - log_claude_usage: bool = False

 3. Part 2: A/B harness script that instantiates two ResearchJob objects (flags-off vs flags-on), runs serially with 30s delay, reads response.usage via the new log_claude_usage flag, computes real costs,

 writes to /tmp/scout_ab_results.jsonl , handles exceptions, enforces cost ceiling.

 Three parts, three commits, no agent/main.py changes, no v2 file, real measurement instead of estimates, numerical success gates, defensive rollout.

 ⏺ Wait — re-reading the usage logging design I realize thread-local storage has a concurrency bug. extract_from_multiple is called via run_in_executor (confirmed at tools/research_engine.py:707 ), which means it

 runs in the thread pool executor, NOT the main asyncio thread. Setting threading.local() in ResearchJob.run() (main thread) creates a TLS entry for the main thread that the executor thread never sees. Logging

 would silently no-op. Fixing.

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit

 rate but dominates when it hits (mean 28.6 contacts). L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low hit rate (10-35%) and low mean yield — dead weight candidates. And territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment is failing on 10

 of 20 jobs because the state field in the Research Log is truncated ("Te", "Oh", "Ok" instead of "TX", "OH", "OK") — that's a data quality issue in the log, not an engine problem, but it means a size-based

 tier system would misfire.

 a small quality sacrifice I shouldn't take in Round 1. Tightening the plan.

 ⏺ Self-review pass complete. Plan tightened — Part 1 (C) early-exit removed (quality risk), cost reduction estimate corrected from 15-35% to 10-25% (matches the actual 3 wins), budget math updated to show Round

 ⎿  Tool use rejected

 ⏺ Understood. Going to hold the full plan in head first, then rewrite from scratch.

 ---

 Thinking out loud before I touch the plan file — what's actually wrong with v1 of this plan:

 The architecture is wrong. I chose "subclass Research Job as Research JobV2 " reflexively because it sounded clean. It isn't. Overriding run() means copying the 50-line phase orchestration because I can't modify

 it mid-flow from a subclass. Overriding _layer15_email_verification means copying 200+ lines to change one if statement near the end. That's not isolation — it's code duplication masquerading as encapsulation.

 The right shape is feature flags in v1 itself, defaulting to old behavior. A/B test becomes: instantiate two ResearchJob objects, one with flags on, one with flags off. No v2 file. Steven's "preserve the old

 way" constraint is satisfied because defaults-off flags preserve byte-for-byte current behavior. I should not have defaulted to subclass-for-isolation without thinking about what isolation actually costs.

 Part 3 (Telegram command) is scope creep. The A/B harness is a local development tool — I run it from Claude Code via .venv/bin/python . Steven doesn't need a /research_ab Telegram command because he's not the

 one running A/B tests. Round 1 shouldn't touch agent/main.py at all. That removes an entire commit and reduces production-path risk.

 The cost estimation is a guess masquerading as a measurement. I wrote "~$0.045/page" from memory. Real Claude API calls return response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens — the SDK gives us

 exact token counts on every call. The research engine doesn't log them today, which means my A/B harness would "estimate" costs from a formula rather than reading actual billing-grade numbers. The foundation

 Round 1 needs is token logging, not cost formulas. Instrument contact_extractor.extract_contacts to capture response.usage on every call, log it with the job, and the A/B harness reads the real numbers.

 The verification plan is vague. "Sanity test — instantiate and run" isn't a test, it's a smoke check. Each specific behavior change needs a unit test with the failure case explicitly constructed: Part 0's

 dedup fix needs a test where page 1 returns "John Smith no email" and page 2 returns "John Smith + VERIFIED email" and the assertion is that the final contact has the email. Part 1(A)'s URL dedup needs three

 cases (exact duplicate, trailing slash, query string difference) because real URLs have those variations. Part 1(B)'s L15 skip needs two cases (14 VERIFIED → runs, 15 VERIFIED → skipped). Without these, the

 integration test against 5 targets is checking the wrong thing — it's measuring yield, not correctness.

 Success metrics are floors, not gates. "≥95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts" is directionally right but not numerically specific enough to serve as a ship/no-ship gate. A senior review asks "what EXACTLY fails

 Round 1 acceptance?" and the plan should answer that with three per-target predicates joined by AND: verified_contacts ≥ v1 × 0.95 AND cost ≤ v1 × 0.90 AND wall_clock ≤ v1 × 1.05 . All three, every target. One

 miss blocks Round 2.

 Part 0 is safer than I said but the rollout needs a discipline. Modifying shared code that both the default engine AND the new flagged paths depend on is a production-path change. "Very low risk" is too glib.

 The discipline: Part 0 ships FIRST, standalone, with unit tests, and gets integration-tested on one of the 5 targets (v1-pre vs v1-post) BEFORE any other changes touch the file. If the fix produces fewer

 verified contacts than the bug, the fix is wrong. Only after Part 0 is green does Part 1 start.

 Round 1 doesn't hit the budget and I should say why that's fine. $0.15-0.20/job savings against a $0.55/job blended baseline is ~25% cost reduction. At ~100 jobs/week saturation that's $55/week → $41/week.

 Still 64% over the $25/week cap. Round 1's job is NOT to solve the budget problem — it's to (a) fix the two silent quality bugs, (b) ship the cheap low-risk wins, (c) build the A/B harness that validates v2

 intrinsic behavior, and (d) create the foundation for Round 2 where the real cost levers live (entity cache, Claude batching, model swap on high-confidence pages, job-level parallelism). Round 1 is a

 prerequisite, not the endgame. The plan needs to state this explicitly with honest numbers, not hand-wave with "foundation."

 The missing things a senior reviewer would catch:

 1. No .gitignore entry for the A/B results file — it'll get committed accidentally

 2. No rate-limit handling in the A/B harness — two serial runs of ~57 Serper queries back-to-back can clip the 60/min rate limit; need a ≥30s delay between v1 and v2

 3. No handling of the case where v1 or v2 raises mid-run — harness should capture the exception and record it, not crash

 4. No way to run the A/B offline against cached results — every A/B run costs real API money even when debugging the harness itself. Need a fixture mode.

 5. No stopping condition — if v2 finds 0 contacts on a target while v1 finds 40, the A/B run should halt immediately and surface the target

 6. No cost ceiling — what if the harness itself costs $10 to run? Need a cap.

 7. The L15 Step 5 threshold ( 15 VERIFIED ) is a guess, not tuned. Real data showed median with_email is 5 and mean is 22 — a 15-threshold will rarely trigger. A data-driven threshold would be the median plus a

 margin, which for this distribution might be closer to 10.

 8. response.usage only works if we capture it per-call. If extract_from_multiple batches calls (it doesn't today), the attribution gets fuzzy.

 What's actually strong and should be preserved:

 - The 5 test districts (Cypress-Fairbanks, Cincinnati, Conejo Valley, Lake Zurich, Waverly) are the right set. Keep.

 - The measurement data (per-layer aggregates, L16/L17 variance, dedup bugs) is solid and anchors everything. Keep.

 - The Part 0 shared dedup fix is the right first move. Keep (with better test discipline).

 - The "no subclass, just preserve behavior" instinct is right — I just picked the wrong mechanism.

 - The Round 2/3 deferrals are correct — cache + batching + parallelism don't belong in Round 1.

 - The 5-target A/B verification against v1 is the right validation step.

 What's actually the right Round 1:

 1. Part 0: Dedup fix in contact_extractor.extract_from_multiple and ResearchJob._merge_contacts , with unit tests and pre-merge integration check against one target.

 2. Part 1: Three feature flags in ResearchJob.__init__ , all defaulting to preserved-v1-behavior:

 - enable_url_dedup: bool = False

 - l15_step5_skip_threshold: int | None = None

 - log_claude_usage: bool = False

 3. Part 2: A/B harness script that instantiates two ResearchJob objects (flags-off vs flags-on), runs serially with 30s delay, reads response.usage via the new log_claude_usage flag, computes real costs,

 writes to /tmp/scout_ab_results.jsonl , handles exceptions, enforces cost ceiling.

 Three parts, three commits, no agent/main.py changes, no v2 file, real measurement instead of estimates, numerical success gates, defensive rollout.

 runs in the thread pool executor, NOT the main asyncio thread. Setting threading.local() in ResearchJob.run() (main thread) creates a TLS entry for the main thread that the executor thread never sees. Logging

 would silently no-op. Fixing.

 ⏺ The data is dramatically clearer now. The real insight isn't "tier by district size" — it's "tier by layer reliability." L1, L2, L3 have 75-90% hit rates and are the reliable workhorses. L16 has only 25% hit

 rate but dominates when it hits (mean 28.6 contacts). L4/L5/L11/L12/L13/L20 are low hit rate (10-35%) and low mean yield — dead weight candidates. And territory_data.lookup_district_enrollment is failing on 10

 of 20 jobs because the state field in the Research Log is truncated ("Te", "Oh", "Ok" instead of "TX", "OH", "OK") — that's a data quality issue in the log, not an engine problem, but it means a size-based

 tier system would misfire.

 a small quality sacrifice I shouldn't take in Round 1. Tightening the plan.

 ⏺ Understood. Going to hold the full plan in head first, then rewrite from scratch.

 ---

 Thinking out loud before I touch the plan file — what's actually wrong with v1 of this plan:

 The architecture is wrong. I chose "subclass Research Job as Research JobV2 " reflexively because it sounded clean. It isn't. Overriding run() means copying the 50-line phase orchestration because I can't modify

 it mid-flow from a subclass. Overriding _layer15_email_verification means copying 200+ lines to change one if statement near the end. That's not isolation — it's code duplication masquerading as encapsulation.

 The right shape is feature flags in v1 itself, defaulting to old behavior. A/B test becomes: instantiate two ResearchJob objects, one with flags on, one with flags off. No v2 file. Steven's "preserve the old

 way" constraint is satisfied because defaults-off flags preserve byte-for-byte current behavior. I should not have defaulted to subclass-for-isolation without thinking about what isolation actually costs.

 Part 3 (Telegram command) is scope creep. The A/B harness is a local development tool — I run it from Claude Code via .venv/bin/python . Steven doesn't need a /research_ab Telegram command because he's not the

 one running A/B tests. Round 1 shouldn't touch agent/main.py at all. That removes an entire commit and reduces production-path risk.

 The cost estimation is a guess masquerading as a measurement. I wrote "~$0.045/page" from memory. Real Claude API calls return response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens — the SDK gives us

 exact token counts on every call. The research engine doesn't log them today, which means my A/B harness would "estimate" costs from a formula rather than reading actual billing-grade numbers. The foundation

 Round 1 needs is token logging, not cost formulas. Instrument contact_extractor.extract_contacts to capture response.usage on every call, log it with the job, and the A/B harness reads the real numbers.

 The verification plan is vague. "Sanity test — instantiate and run" isn't a test, it's a smoke check. Each specific behavior change needs a unit test with the failure case explicitly constructed: Part 0's

 dedup fix needs a test where page 1 returns "John Smith no email" and page 2 returns "John Smith + VERIFIED email" and the assertion is that the final contact has the email. Part 1(A)'s URL dedup needs three

 cases (exact duplicate, trailing slash, query string difference) because real URLs have those variations. Part 1(B)'s L15 skip needs two cases (14 VERIFIED → runs, 15 VERIFIED → skipped). Without these, the

 integration test against 5 targets is checking the wrong thing — it's measuring yield, not correctness.

 Success metrics are floors, not gates. "≥95% of v1's VERIFIED contacts" is directionally right but not numerically specific enough to serve as a ship/no-ship gate. A senior review asks "what EXACTLY fails

 Round 1 acceptance?" and the plan should answer that with three per-target predicates joined by AND: verified_contacts ≥ v1 × 0.95 AND cost ≤ v1 × 0.90 AND wall_clock ≤ v1 × 1.05 . All three, every target. One

 miss blocks Round 2.

 Part 0 is safer than I said but the rollout needs a discipline. Modifying shared code that both the default engine AND the new flagged paths depend on is a production-path change. "Very low risk" is too glib.

 The discipline: Part 0 ships FIRST, standalone, with unit tests, and gets integration-tested on one of the 5 targets (v1-pre vs v1-post) BEFORE any other changes touch the file. If the fix produces fewer

 verified contacts than the bug, the fix is wrong. Only after Part 0 is green does Part 1 start.

 Round 1 doesn't hit the budget and I should say why that's fine. $0.15-0.20/job savings against a $0.55/job blended baseline is ~25% cost reduction. At ~100 jobs/week saturation that's $55/week → $41/week.

 Still 64% over the $25/week cap. Round 1's job is NOT to solve the budget problem — it's to (a) fix the two silent quality bugs, (b) ship the cheap low-risk wins, (c) build the A/B harness that validates v2

 intrinsic behavior, and (d) create the foundation for Round 2 where the real cost levers live (entity cache, Claude batching, model swap on high-confidence pages, job-level parallelism). Round 1 is a

 prerequisite, not the endgame. The plan needs to state this explicitly with honest numbers, not hand-wave with "foundation."

 The missing things a senior reviewer would catch:

 1. No .gitignore entry for the A/B results file — it'll get committed accidentally

 2. No rate-limit handling in the A/B harness — two serial runs of ~57 Serper queries back-to-back can clip the 60/min rate limit; need a ≥30s delay between v1 and v2

 3. No handling of the case where v1 or v2 raises mid-run — harness should capture the exception and record it, not crash

 4. No way to run the A/B offline against cached results — every A/B run costs real API money even when debugging the harness itself. Need a fixture mode.

 5. No stopping condition — if v2 finds 0 contacts on a target while v1 finds 40, the A/B run should halt immediately and surface the target

 6. No cost ceiling — what if the harness itself costs $10 to run? Need a cap.

 7. The L15 Step 5 threshold ( 15 VERIFIED ) is a guess, not tuned. Real data showed median with_email is 5 and mean is 22 — a 15-threshold will rarely trigger. A data-driven threshold would be the median plus a

 margin, which for this distribution might be closer to 10.

 8. response.usage only works if we capture it per-call. If extract_from_multiple batches calls (it doesn't today), the attribution gets fuzzy.

 What's actually strong and should be preserved:

 - The 5 test districts (Cypress-Fairbanks, Cincinnati, Conejo Valley, Lake Zurich, Waverly) are the right set. Keep.

 - The measurement data (per-layer aggregates, L16/L17 variance, dedup bugs) is solid and anchors everything. Keep.

 - The Part 0 shared dedup fix is the right first move. Keep (with better test discipline).

 - The "no subclass, just preserve behavior" instinct is right — I just picked the wrong mechanism.

 - The Round 2/3 deferrals are correct — cache + batching + parallelism don't belong in Round 1.

 - The 5-target A/B verification against v1 is the right validation step.

 What's actually the right Round 1:

 1. Part 0: Dedup fix in contact_extractor.extract_from_multiple and ResearchJob._merge_contacts , with unit tests and pre-merge integration check against one target.

 2. Part 1: Three feature flags in ResearchJob.__init__ , all defaulting to preserved-v1-behavior:

 - enable_url_dedup: bool = False

 - l15_step5_skip_threshold: int | None = None

 - log_claude_usage: bool = False

 3. Part 2: A/B harness script that instantiates two ResearchJob objects (flags-off vs flags-on), runs serially with 30s delay, reads response.usage via the new log_claude_usage flag, computes real costs,

 writes to /tmp/scout_ab_results.jsonl , handles exceptions, enforces cost ceiling.

 Three parts, three commits, no agent/main.py changes, no v2 file, real measurement instead of estimates, numerical success gates, defensive rollout.

 ⏺ Wait — re-reading the usage logging design I realize thread-local storage has a concurrency bug. extract_from_multiple is called via run_in_executor (confirmed at tools/research_engine.py:707 ), which means it

 runs in the thread pool executor, NOT the main asyncio thread. Setting threading.local() in ResearchJob.run() (main thread) creates a TLS entry for the main thread that the executor thread never sees. Logging

 would silently no-op. Fixing.
